test_id_1.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and mainly points providers back to the eMedNY pharmacy billing guides rather than stating the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule itself [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-23 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01].
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule directly; it says, "Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance." [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01] Earlier policy guidance says, "Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] It also states, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance is more specific for dual eligible pharmacy claims: "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] It also says, "The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations," and "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — 2025-10-01] The manual further states, "Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:23 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy-related billing, because policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual says pharmacies must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations rather than allowing unrestricted Medicaid billing: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and the manual says the "total Medicare/Medicaid payment ... will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy excerpts are broader and less operational, with the newest policy source only directing providers to the eMedNY manual for "additional program policy and billing guidance," while the manual gives the actual payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-23 — 2025-10-01].

The older policy excerpt specifically says, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members," whereas the manual instead focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for dual eligible members rather than repeating that blanket billing restriction [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-23 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy-related billing, because policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual says pharmacies must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations rather than allowing unrestricted Medicaid billing: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and the manual says the "total Medicare/Medicaid payment ... will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The policy excerpts are broader and less operational, with the newest policy source only directing providers to the eMedNY manual for "additional program policy and billing guidance," while the manual gives the actual payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-23 — 2025-10-01].

The older policy excerpt specifically says, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members," whereas the manual instead focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for dual eligible members rather than repeating that blanket billing restriction [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-23 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_2.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source is dated 2024-12-01 and is consistent in direction, but it is older than the manual guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says practitioners should refer to the eMedNY provider manual for additional billing guidance, and it directs providers to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost of the drug dosage administered and maintain documentation. Earlier policy guidance for dual eligible billing says Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for dual enrollees. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed what the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made; Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. It also says NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from providers enrolled under federal and State law, and that Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to dual eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources require Medicare to be addressed before Medicaid for dual eligible pharmacy claims: policy says "Medicare must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual says Medicaid-enrolled providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources treat Medicaid payment as limited in dual eligible situations: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort," while the manual caps combined Medicare/Medicaid payment at what would have been received for a Medicaid-only patient. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The policy excerpts are more general and point readers to the provider manual for billing details, saying "providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and that practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages for additional policy and billing guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]

The provider manual gives more operational detail for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including Medicare enrollment requirements and the rule that combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, which is not stated in the policy excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]
comparison: Both sources require Medicare to be addressed before Medicaid for dual eligible pharmacy claims: policy says "Medicare must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual says Medicaid-enrolled providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]

[Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]

prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

It also says NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from providers enrolled under federal and State law, and that Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to dual eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources require Medicare to be addressed before Medicaid for dual eligible pharmacy claims: policy says \"Medicare must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" and the manual says Medicaid-enrolled providers must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01]", "Both sources treat Medicaid payment as limited in dual eligible situations: policy says \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort,\" while the manual caps combined Medicare/Medicaid payment at what would have been received for a Medicaid-only patient. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are more general and point readers to the provider manual for billing details, saying \"providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and that practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages for additional policy and billing guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]", "The provider manual gives more operational detail for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including Medicare enrollment requirements and the rule that combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, which is not stated in the policy excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]"]}
test_id_2.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_2.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_2.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_3.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claim guidance and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source is dated 2024-02-01 and is older; it points providers to the pharmacy billing guide and related manual materials rather than giving the detailed dual-eligible claim rule itself.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule directly; it says, "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and also to the ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01]. Older policy guidance states, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]. Another policy excerpt says, "Through coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says, "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It also states, "the New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations" and that "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy claims as involving Medicare coordination rather than simple Medicaid-only billing: policy says dual eligible members "will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" and that Medicaid pays through coordination of benefits for certain claims [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01], while the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment rules apply and Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members must be submitted and accepted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations: policy says Medicaid will pay "the patient responsibility" or "up to the Medicaid allowed amounts" in specified circumstances [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01], and the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" what would have been paid for a Medicaid-only patient and that no additional payment is made if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The newest policy excerpt is indirect and cross-references other manuals for pharmacy claim instructions, saying providers should refer to the billing guidelines and ProDUR/ECCA manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], while the newest provider manual gives the operative payment rule for dual eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01].

Older policy language is narrower and more categorical, stating that "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" and that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], whereas the provider manual focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for dual eligible members rather than saying pharmacies can only bill non-dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy claims as involving Medicare coordination rather than simple Medicaid-only billing: policy says dual eligible members "will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" and that Medicaid pays through coordination of benefits for certain claims [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01; New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01], while the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment rules apply and Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members must be submitted and accepted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations: policy says Medicaid will pay "the patient responsibility" or "up to the Medicaid allowed amounts" in specified circumstances [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01], and the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" what would have been paid for a Medicaid-only patient and that no additional payment is made if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The newest policy excerpt is indirect and cross-references other manuals for pharmacy claim instructions, saying providers should refer to the billing guidelines and ProDUR/ECCA manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01], while the newest provider manual gives the operative payment rule for dual eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01].

Older policy language is narrower and more categorical, stating that "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" and that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], whereas the provider manual focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for dual eligible members rather than saying pharmacies can only bill non-dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.

drug claims:

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.

Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under

appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_4.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_4.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_4.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_5.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and is more general, while the provider manual gives the current Medicare/Medicaid payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt is general and says providers should use eMedNY pharmacy billing guidance for pharmacy claims: "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and "Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. For dual eligible billing, the policy excerpts say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]. The older 2021 update also says "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" and that "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance is specific to dual eligible pharmacy claims and says "the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made"; it also says "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It further states that "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]. The same manual also says NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so that dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as part of the dual eligible pharmacy claim process: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay as if the member were Medicaid-only without regard to Medicare: policy says Medicaid is "the payer of last resort," and the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts are broader and older, emphasizing billing order and a 2021 statement that "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members," while the newer manual gives current payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more operational and specific, stating that Medicare/Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" and that pharmacies must have Medicare enrollment for dual eligible coverage, whereas the policy excerpts do not state those payment-in-full or enrollment requirements in the same detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare as part of the dual eligible pharmacy claim process: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay as if the member were Medicaid-only without regard to Medicare: policy says Medicaid is "the payer of last resort," and the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are broader and older, emphasizing billing order and a 2021 statement that "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members," while the newer manual gives current payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more operational and specific, stating that Medicare/Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" and that pharmacies must have Medicare enrollment for dual eligible coverage, whereas the policy excerpts do not state those payment-in-full or enrollment requirements in the same detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023].

Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.

Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
5.0 Utilization Management Programs
Eligibility
Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of the Medicaid member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and is more general, while the provider manual gives the current Medicare/Medicaid payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible pharmacy claims.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt is general and says providers should use eMedNY pharmacy billing guidance for pharmacy claims: \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and \"Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. For dual eligible billing, the policy excerpts say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]. The older 2021 update also says \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" and that \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance is specific to dual eligible pharmacy claims and says \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made\"; it also says \"the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It further states that \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]. The same manual also says NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so that dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources treat Medicare as part of the dual eligible pharmacy claim process: policy says \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" and the manual says providers must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023;
