New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are broader and older, emphasizing billing order and a 2021 statement that \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,\" while the newer manual gives current payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The provider manual is more operational and specific, stating that Medicare/Medicaid payment \"must be accepted as payment in full\" and that pharmacies must have Medicare enrollment for dual eligible coverage, whereas the policy excerpts do not state those payment-in-full or enrollment requirements in the same detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]."]}
test_id_5.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_5.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_5.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_6.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is secondary to other liable coverage for pharmacy claims, but the provider manual adds that a responsible third party must pay first and that total Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort"; it also states that "all other available resources [must] be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] Earlier policy language similarly says that "Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility" for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims or pay up to Medicaid allowed amounts for certain excluded drug classes, but the excerpts do not further define dual-eligible pharmacy primary payer responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that "all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that if "there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01] The manual also says that for Medicare/Medicaid claims, "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources place Medicaid after other liable coverage for pharmacy claims: policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before NYS Medicaid and that Medicaid is "the payer of last resort," while the manual says "all available resources" must be used before Medicaid pays. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources require primary payer responsibility to be satisfied by another liable payer before Medicaid payment is considered: policy says other resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment, and the manual says a "responsible third party" that is the "primary payor" "must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual is more specific about dual-eligible pharmacy claims because it addresses Medicare/Medicaid payment limits, stating that total payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made"; the policy excerpt does not include that payment-cap detail. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]

The policy excerpt uses broader coordination-of-benefits language—"applicable third parties" and "payer of last resort"—while the provider manual expressly identifies a "responsible third party" as the "primary payor" who "must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]
comparison: Both sources place Medicaid after other liable coverage for pharmacy claims: policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before NYS Medicaid and that Medicaid is "the payer of last resort," while the manual says "all available resources" must be used before Medicaid pays. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources require primary payer responsibility to be satisfied by another liable payer before Medicaid payment is considered: policy says other resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment, and the manual says a "responsible third party" that is the "primary payor" "must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

The provider manual is more specific about dual-eligible pharmacy claims because it addresses Medicare/Medicaid payment limits, stating that total payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made"; the policy excerpt does not include that payment-cap detail. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]

The policy excerpt uses broader coordination-of-benefits language—"applicable third parties" and "payer of last resort"—while the provider manual expressly identifies a "responsible third party" as the "primary payor" who "must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is secondary to other liable coverage and that a responsible primary payor must pay first for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, but the provider manual states this more directly and in more current terms. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance also says Medicaid pays only after coordination of benefits, including "the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims" or up to Medicaid allowed amounts for certain excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that all available resources must be used before Medicaid pays and that a responsible third party must pay first: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment... If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members, and that Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid and treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: "bill applicable third parties... before billing NYS Medicaid" and "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources place primary payment responsibility on the responsible third party/primary payor rather than Medicaid: "If there is a responsible third party... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more explicit about dual-eligible pharmacy claims and primary payor responsibility, stating that "a responsible third party... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the rule more generally as billing liable third parties before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual includes Medicare-specific dual-eligible pharmacy instructions, including that providers "are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" and that combined Medicare/Medicaid payment limits apply [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts only say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and mention coordination of benefits without those detailed Medicare billing rules [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021].
comparison: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid and treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: "bill applicable third parties... before billing NYS Medicaid" and "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources place primary payment responsibility on the responsible third party/primary payor rather than Medicaid: "If there is a responsible third party... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more explicit about dual-eligible pharmacy claims and primary payor responsibility, stating that "a responsible third party... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the rule more generally as billing liable third parties before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual includes Medicare-specific dual-eligible pharmacy instructions, including that providers "are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" and that combined Medicare/Medicaid payment limits apply [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts only say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and mention coordination of benefits without those detailed Medicare billing rules [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_7.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_7.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_7.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is secondary to other liable coverage and that the responsible third party must pay first, but the provider manual adds more specific payment-limit language for Medicare/Medicaid pharmacy claims. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort and providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025] It also states that through COB, Medicaid pays only after other coverage and, in earlier policy language, "Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts" when drugs are excluded from the TPL plan, including Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 1, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that all available resources must be used before Medicaid pays and that if a responsible third party is the primary payor, "that responsible third party must pay first." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025] For Medicare/Medicaid pharmacy claims, the manual further says the combined payment to the pharmacy "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place Medicaid after other liable coverage and require billing other third parties first: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025] and "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].

Both sources say a responsible third party must pay first when it is the primary payor: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025] and "that responsible third party must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about pharmacy claim payment limits for Medicare/Medicaid claims, stating the total payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that any Medicare and Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025], while the policy update is broader and focuses on payer order and Medicaid as payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025].

The policy excerpt includes an older carve-out-style statement that Medicaid may pay "the patient responsibility" or "up to the Medicaid allowed amounts" for drugs excluded from the TPL plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 1, 2021], but the newer provider manual excerpt does not use that wording and instead frames the issue as primary-payor-first plus payment caps [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,18 — Oct 1, 2025].
comparison: Both sources place Medicaid after other liable coverage and require billing other third parties first: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025] and "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].

Both sources say a responsible third party must pay first when it is the primary payor: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025] and "that responsible third party must pay first" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific about pharmacy claim payment limits for Medicare/Medicaid claims, stating the total payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that any Medicare and Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025], while the policy update is broader and focuses on payer order and Medicaid as payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025].

The policy excerpt includes an older carve-out-style statement that Medicaid may pay "the patient responsibility" or "up to the Medicaid allowed amounts" for drugs excluded from the TPL plan, including Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 1, 2021], but the newer provider manual excerpt does not use that wording and instead frames the issue as primary-payor-first plus payment caps [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,18 — Oct 1, 2025].

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].", "Both sources say a responsible third party must pay first when it is the primary payor: \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 1, 2025] and \"that responsible third party must pay first\" [Pharmacy Manual - "For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.

test_id_9.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_9.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is payer of last resort and that a responsible third party must pay first for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, but the provider manual adds more detailed payment-limit language for Medicare/Medicaid claims. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid, because "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" and "providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance similarly states that "Through coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] For Medicare/Medicaid pharmacy claims, the manual adds that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: policy says providers must "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid," and the manual says "all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place primary payment responsibility on the other payer first: policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," while the manual says "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying ... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific about payment limits for Medicare/Medicaid claims, stating that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that such payment "must be accepted as payment in full"; the policy excerpt does not include this cap. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt frames the rule more generally as billing liable third parties before NYS Medicaid and identifies Medicaid as "the payer of last resort," while the provider manual expressly uses the term "primary payor" and says the responsible third party "must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: policy says providers must "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid," and the manual says "all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place primary payment responsibility on the other payer first: policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," while the manual says "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying ... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific about payment limits for Medicare/Medicaid claims, stating that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that such payment "must be accepted as payment in full"; the policy excerpt does not include this cap. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt frames the rule more generally as billing liable third parties before NYS Medicaid and identifies Medicaid as "the payer of last resort," while the provider manual expressly uses the term "primary payor" and says the responsible third party "must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_10.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."

test_id_10.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-05-01 still says providers cannot directly recover Medicare Advantage co-payment/co-insurance from dually eligible individuals, but the provider manual adds the payment-in-full and no-balance-billing framing.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says a Medicare Part C provider must report the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amount, and that the provider “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co- insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] Earlier policy guidance also emphasizes that providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims and warns against inflating patient responsibility amounts. [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5-6 — Oct 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider “will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient,” and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, “no additional payment will be made.” It also states that “the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full” and that a pharmacy provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] The same manual also limits Medicaid payment for dual-eligible members to certain excluded drugs under Part D. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place the cost-sharing burden on the provider side rather than allowing direct recovery from the dual-eligible member: policy says the provider “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co- insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” and the manual says the provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources require proper handling of Medicare cost-sharing on Medicaid claims: policy requires providers to “accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims,” and the manual ties payment to Medicare/Medicaid coordination and says the combined payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5-6 — Oct 01, 2019; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpt is narrower and specifically addresses Medicare Part C/Medicare Advantage co-payment and co-insurance recovery from “NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” while the provider manual uses broader payment language and says the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds a payment cap and no-additional-payment rule—“will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient” and “If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made”—which is not stated in the policy excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources place the cost-sharing burden on the provider side rather than allowing direct recovery from the dual-eligible member: policy says the provider “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co- insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” and the manual says the provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts” from the member. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources require proper handling of Medicare cost-sharing on Medicaid claims: policy requires providers to “accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid claims,” and the manual ties payment to Medicare/Medicaid coordination and says the combined payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10:5-6 — Oct 01, 2019; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt is narrower and specifically addresses Medicare Part C/Medicare Advantage co-payment and co-insurance recovery from “NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” while the provider manual uses broader payment language and says the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds a payment cap and no-additional-payment rule—“will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient” and “If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made”—which is not stated in the policy excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
