test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_12.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2024-05-01 is consistent on the core point that providers may not recover co-payment/co-insurance from dual-eligible members, but the provider manual adds the stronger current payment-in-full and billing-limit language.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says that for Medicare Advantage/Part C claims, "A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" and must report the deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts on the claim [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. Older policy guidance also states that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made"; it also says "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a pharmacy provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also limits Medicaid billing for dual-eligible members by stating, "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" and that Medicaid only pays for certain excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources prohibit shifting Medicare cost-sharing to the dual-eligible member: policy says a provider "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], and the manual says the payment "must be accepted as payment in full" and a provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources treat Medicare as primary before Medicaid for dual-eligible billing: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], while the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment rules apply and Medicaid is only secondary within the stated limits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is more explicit about the payment consequence: it says "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and caps total payment at the Medicaid-only amount [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt focuses on claim reporting and says providers cannot recover co-payment/co-insurance directly from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01].

The policy excerpt is narrower and specifically tied to "Medicare Part C" claims and reporting of "deductible amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], whereas the provider manual excerpt addresses broader pharmacy billing rules for Medicare Part B and Part D dual-eligible situations and does not use the same Part C-specific claim-reporting language [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources prohibit shifting Medicare cost-sharing to the dual-eligible member: policy says a provider "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], and the manual says the payment "must be accepted as payment in full" and a provider "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources treat Medicare as primary before Medicaid for dual-eligible billing: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], while the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment rules apply and Medicaid is only secondary within the stated limits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is more explicit about the payment consequence: it says "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and caps total payment at the Medicaid-only amount [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt focuses on claim reporting and says providers cannot recover co-payment/co-insurance directly from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01].

The policy excerpt is narrower and specifically tied to "Medicare Part C" claims and reporting of "deductible amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], whereas the provider manual excerpt addresses broader pharmacy billing rules for Medicare Part B and Part D dual-eligible situations and does not use the same Part C-specific claim-reporting language [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].

• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:

_mmc.pdf.

guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_12.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2024-05-01 is consistent on the core point that providers may not recover co-payment/co-insurance from dual-eligible members, but the provider manual adds the stronger current payment-in-full and billing-limit language. ", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says that for Medicare Advantage/Part C claims, \"A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals\" and must report the deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts on the claim [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01]. Older policy guidance also states that \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort\" and that \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that \"If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made\"; it also says \"The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" and that a pharmacy provider \"cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also limits Medicaid billing for dual-eligible members by stating, \"It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below\" and that Medicaid only pays for certain excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources prohibit shifting Medicare cost-sharing to the dual-eligible member: policy says a provider \"cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], and the manual says the payment \"must be accepted as payment in full\" and a provider \"cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources treat Medicare as primary before Medicaid for dual-eligible billing: policy says \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort\" and \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], while the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment rules apply and Medicaid is only secondary within the stated limits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more explicit about the payment consequence: it says \"The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" and caps total payment at the Medicaid-only amount [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt focuses on claim reporting and says providers cannot recover co-payment/co-insurance directly from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01].", "The policy excerpt is narrower and specifically tied to \"Medicare Part C\" claims and reporting of \"deductible amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — 2024-05-01], whereas the provider manual excerpt addresses broader pharmacy billing rules for Medicare Part B and Part D dual-eligible situations and does not use the same Part C-specific claim-reporting language [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01]."]}
test_id_12.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_12.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_12.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_13.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The policy excerpts are newer only through 2025-07-01 and mainly address dual-eligible billing/payment rules, while the provider manual more directly states how copayments are handled and collected. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 1, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" and must report the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts on the claim. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024] The policy also states that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 1, 2023] The July 2025 policy update notes that "duals are subject to Medicare rules," but it does not add a specific copayment collection rule in the excerpt provided. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 1, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt says "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025] It also says "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025] For copayments, the manual says "Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other legal means" and that "Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 1, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicaid/Medicare coordination as limiting what can be collected or billed from dual-eligible members, with policy saying providers "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" and the manual saying payment "must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025]

Both sources include Medicare-related payment restrictions for duals, with policy requiring Medicare to be billed before Medicaid and the manual stating that Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that no additional payment is made when Medicare pays more. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 1, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025]
differences: The provider manual gives the clearest current copayment collection instruction: "Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments" by requesting them, sending bills, or other legal means, while the policy excerpt only says providers cannot attempt to recover copayment or coinsurance amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals in the Medicare Part C context. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 1, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]

The policy excerpts focus on billing order and claim reporting for dual eligibles—"Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and providers must report deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts—whereas the provider manual focuses on payment acceptance and collection mechanics, including that copayments may be pursued and that the Medicaid claim amount must not be reduced by the collected copayment. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 1, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 1, 2025]
comparison: Both sources treat Medicaid/Medicare coordination as limiting what can be collected or billed from dual-eligible members, with policy saying providers "cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals" and the manual saying payment "must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025]

Both sources include Medicare-related payment restrictions for duals, with policy requiring Medicare to be billed before Medicaid and the manual stating that Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that no additional payment is made when Medicare pays more. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 1, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025]

The provider manual gives the clearest current copayment collection instruction: "Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments" by requesting them, sending bills, or other legal means, while the policy excerpt only says providers cannot attempt to recover copayment or coinsurance amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals in the Medicare Part C context. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 1, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 1, 2024]

The policy excerpts focus on billing order and claim reporting for dual eligibles—"Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and providers must report deductible, co-insurance, co-payment, and paid amounts—whereas the provider manual focuses on payment acceptance and collection mechanics, including that copayments may be pursued and that the Medicaid claim amount must not be reduced by the collected copayment. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 1, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 1, 2025]

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_14.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for co-payment responsibility and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-05-01 says providers cannot attempt to recover Medicare Advantage co-payment or co-insurance directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals, while the provider manual adds that Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full and that providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through billing or other legal means. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]

[New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]. The older policy excerpt only states that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid," without further co-payment collection detail. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt says that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient," that "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made," and that "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." It also says a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Me... [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. A related provider manual excerpt adds that "Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other legal means," while also stating that providers must not reduce the Medicaid claim by the copayment collected from a Medicaid member. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:40 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare/Medicaid coordination as limiting what can be collected from dual-eligible members and require payment handling tied to Medicare and Medicaid rules: policy says providers cannot recover certain co-payment/co-insurance amounts directly from dual eligibles, and the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources address co-payment-related billing in the dual-eligible context rather than treating the member as a standard Medicaid-only patient: policy requires reporting the Medicare Advantage deductible/co-insurance/co-payment amounts, and the manual discusses outstanding copayments and claim handling. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:40 — Oct 01, 2025]

[New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.

Patient Groups (APGs) Medicaid Fee-for-Service Provider Manual – Policy and Billing Guidelines, located

.pdf.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_14.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_14.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_14.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_15.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual-eligible co-payment responsibility and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source is dated 2024-05-01, and both sources say the provider cannot seek to recover co-payment/coinsurance from the dual-eligible member, but the provider manual adds the stronger payment-in-full rule and Medicare Part B-specific language. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance says a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals” and must “accept the Medicare ...” payment terms as part of billing the claim. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] Earlier policy also states that for dual eligible enrollees, “Medicaid is the payer of last resort,” and “potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that for Medicare/Medicaid payment, “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full,” and that “a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also requires pharmacies to “submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members,” which frames the co-payment rule as part of Medicare-covered dual-eligible billing. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources prohibit shifting co-payment/coinsurance responsibility to the dual-eligible member: policy says providers “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” and the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicare as the primary payer before Medicaid for dual-eligible billing: policy says “Medicaid is the payer of last resort” and “Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid,” while the manual requires pharmacies to “submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more explicit and current: it says “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full,” while the newest policy excerpt only says providers cannot recover co-payment/coinsurance directly from dual-eligible individuals and does not state the payment-in-full rule in the quoted text. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]

The provider manual specifically addresses “Medicare Part B” deductible/coinsurance recovery, whereas the policy excerpt addresses “Medicare Advantage Plan deductible amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount” and dual-eligible individuals more generally. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]
comparison: Both sources prohibit shifting co-payment/coinsurance responsibility to the dual-eligible member: policy says providers “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” and the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicare as the primary payer before Medicaid for dual-eligible billing: policy says “Medicaid is the payer of last resort” and “Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid,” while the manual requires pharmacies to “submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]

• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.

full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment

test_id_15.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual-eligible co-payment responsibility and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source is dated 2024-05-01, and both sources say the provider cannot seek to recover co-payment/coinsurance from the dual-eligible member, but the provider manual adds the stronger payment-in-full rule and Medicare Part B-specific language. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]", "policy_definition": "Policy guidance says a provider of a Medicare Part C benefit “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals” and must “accept the Medicare ...” payment terms as part of billing the claim. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] Earlier policy also states that for dual eligible enrollees, “Medicaid is the payer of last resort,” and “potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]", "provider_manual_definition": "The provider manual states that for Medicare/Medicaid payment, “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full,” and that “a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also requires pharmacies to “submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members,” which frames the co-payment rule as part of Medicare-covered dual-eligible billing. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources prohibit shifting co-payment/coinsurance responsibility to the dual-eligible member: policy says providers “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” and the manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources treat Medicare as the primary payer before Medicaid for dual-eligible billing: policy says “Medicaid is the payer of last resort” and “Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid,” while the manual requires pharmacies to “submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is more explicit and current: it says “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full,” while the newest policy excerpt only says providers cannot recover co-payment/coinsurance directly from dual-eligible individuals and does not state the payment-in-full rule in the quoted text. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]", "The provider manual specifically addresses “Medicare Part B” deductible/coinsurance recovery, whereas the policy excerpt addresses “Medicare Advantage Plan deductible amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount” and dual-eligible individuals more generally. test_id_15.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_15.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_15.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy excerpts are older (newest dated 2025-02-01) and only address a copayment override, while the provider manual specifically explains when Medicaid may pay for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt only addresses a copayment override: "Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios: In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance)." [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] The provided policy chunks do not define how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs beyond this copayment-related instruction; no Part D-excluded drug coverage rule appears in the excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states: "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] It then limits Medicaid payment to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list where "the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources are Medicaid pharmacy guidance and use exclusion-based language rather than broad coverage language: the policy excerpt refers to an "Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance" override, and the provider manual refers to drugs "specifically excluded by law" from Part D coverage. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources show that Medicaid handling is limited by specific conditions rather than automatic payment: the policy excerpt limits the instruction to removing copayment in listed scenarios, while the provider manual says billing Medicaid for a Medicare Part D member is not appropriate "unless as stated below." [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpt does not define Part D-excluded drug coverage; it only gives a billing override for copayment/coinsurance removal. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]

The provider manual directly addresses Part D-excluded drugs and says Medicaid only pays for drugs on the reimbursable list when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law" from Part D coverage, naming "Select prescription vitamins" and "Certain non-prescription drugs." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources are Medicaid pharmacy guidance and use exclusion-based language rather than broad coverage language: the policy excerpt refers to an "Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance" override, and the provider manual refers to drugs "specifically excluded by law" from Part D coverage. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources show that Medicaid handling is limited by specific conditions rather than automatic payment: the policy excerpt limits the instruction to removing copayment in listed scenarios, while the provider manual says billing Medicaid for a Medicare Part D member is not appropriate "unless as stated below." [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt does not define Part D-excluded drug coverage; it only gives a billing override for copayment/coinsurance removal. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — Feb 01, 2025]

The provider manual directly addresses Part D-excluded drugs and says Medicaid only pays for drugs on the reimbursable list when the drug class is "specifically excluded by law" from Part D coverage, naming "Select prescription vitamins" and "Certain non-prescription drugs." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-03-01 is older and says Medicaid covers only certain Part D-excluded drugs, while the provider manual adds the current billing rule that Medicaid is not appropriate for any Part D member prescription drug unless it falls within that exclusion. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans,” including “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” It also says that for dual-eligible members, claims are limited to those Part D-excluded drugs. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] The older policy excerpt also states that practitioner-administered drugs may be billed through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit, but it does not add any different Part D-exclusion rule. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14:10-11 — Dec 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt says, “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below,” and then limits Medicaid payment to drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans,” such as “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” It also says that “All claims for dual-eligible members” are subject to that rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources limit Medicaid payment to Medicare Part D-excluded drugs rather than general Part D-covered drugs: policy says Medicaid pays only where the drug class is “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans,” and the manual says Medicaid is not appropriate for any Part D member prescription drug “unless as stated below.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources identify the same examples of excluded drug types: “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more explicit and restrictive in billing language, stating, “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below,” whereas the policy excerpt frames the rule as Medicaid paying only for certain excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023]

The policy excerpt includes a broader contextual statement about dual-eligible claims and the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, while the provider manual focuses on current billing guidance for Part D members and dual-eligible claims. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources limit Medicaid payment to Medicare Part D-excluded drugs rather than general Part D-covered drugs: policy says Medicaid pays only where the drug class is “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans,” and the manual says Medicaid is not appropriate for any Part D member prescription drug “unless as stated below.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources identify the same examples of excluded drug types: “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more explicit and restrictive in billing language, stating, “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below,” whereas the policy excerpt frames the rule as Medicaid paying only for certain excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023]

The policy excerpt includes a broader contextual statement about dual-eligible claims and the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, while the provider manual focuses on current billing guidance for Part D members and dual-eligible claims. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
