policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_17.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-03-01 is older and says Medicaid covers only certain Part D-excluded drugs, while the provider manual adds the current billing rule that Medicaid is not appropriate for any Part D member prescription drug unless it falls within that exclusion. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans,” including “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” It also says that for dual-eligible members, claims are limited to those Part D-excluded drugs. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] The older policy excerpt also states that practitioner-administered drugs may be billed through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit, but it does not add any different Part D-exclusion rule. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14:10-11 — Dec 01, 2022]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual excerpt says, “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below,” and then limits Medicaid payment to drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans,” such as “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” It also says that “All claims for dual-eligible members” are subject to that rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources limit Medicaid payment to Medicare Part D-excluded drugs rather than general Part D-covered drugs: policy says Medicaid pays only where the drug class is “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans,” and the manual says Medicaid is not appropriate for any Part D member prescription drug “unless as stated below.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources identify the same examples of excluded drug types: “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is more explicit and restrictive in billing language, stating, “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below,” whereas the policy excerpt frames the rule as Medicaid paying only for certain excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023]", "The policy excerpt includes a broader contextual statement about dual-eligible claims and the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, while the provider manual focuses on current billing guidance for Part D members and dual-eligible claims. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]"]}
test_id_17.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_17.pm_ground_truth: Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_17.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_18.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. It says Medicaid generally does not pay for Medicare Part D members’ prescription drugs unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, while the newest policy source dated 2025-02-01 only provides a copay-override instruction and does not define Part D-excluded drug coverage. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define how Medicare Part D-excluded drugs are handled; it only says, “Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios: In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value ‘04’ (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance).” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01] The provided policy chunks otherwise do not state the Part D-exclusion rule for drug coverage. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states, “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01] It further says, “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources address Medicaid pharmacy handling in a Medicare-related context, but the policy excerpt does so only through a copay override while the provider manual addresses Part D-member billing and coverage. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources are about pharmacy claims/payment mechanics rather than broader clinical coverage policy, with the policy excerpt focused on removing copayment and the provider manual focused on whether Medicaid may be billed for the drug. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual gives the substantive rule for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs: Medicaid pays only when the drug class is “specifically excluded by law” from Part D coverage and the drug is on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01] The policy excerpt does not state that rule and instead only instructs pharmacies on a copay override code. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]

The provider manual says, “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below,” which is a billing prohibition with a narrow exception. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01] The policy excerpt contains no comparable billing prohibition or exception language for Part D-excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]
comparison: Both sources address Medicaid pharmacy handling in a Medicare-related context, but the policy excerpt does so only through a copay override while the provider manual addresses Part D-member billing and coverage. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources are about pharmacy claims/payment mechanics rather than broader clinical coverage policy, with the policy excerpt focused on removing copayment and the provider manual focused on whether Medicaid may be billed for the drug. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]

The provider manual gives the substantive rule for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs: Medicaid pays only when the drug class is “specifically excluded by law” from Part D coverage and the drug is on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01] The policy excerpt does not state that rule and instead only instructs pharmacies on a copay override code. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]

The provider manual says, “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below,” which is a billing prohibition with a narrow exception. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01] The policy excerpt contains no comparable billing prohibition or exception language for Part D-excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.

answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not specifically define Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, but it does state that practitioner-administered drugs may be billed through either the Medical Benefit or Pharmacy Benefit and that certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. "The ‘Provider Manuals’ tab has billing and policy related guidance" and "Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] The older 2022 update similarly says NYS Medicaid recognizes that drugs requiring practitioner administration may be billable through both benefits and that such drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs. "The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit." [New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14:10-11 — Dec 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance directly addresses Medicare Part D-excluded drugs: "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below." It then says, "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] The same excerpt also states that for dual-eligible members, claims are limited to those exceptions: "All claims for dual-eligible members su..." [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie Medicaid pharmacy coverage to the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs / List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. "Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] and "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources recognize that some drugs are payable through Medicaid despite Medicare-related billing rules. "may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] and "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives the specific Medicare Part D rule, while the policy excerpts do not. "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] versus the policy excerpt's broader discussion of practitioner-administered drugs and billing through Medical Benefit or Pharmacy Benefit [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023].

The provider manual expressly limits Medicaid payment to drugs whose class is "specifically excluded by law" from Part D, while the policy excerpts do not state that Part D exclusion standard. "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] and the policy excerpt only says certain practitioner-administered drugs "may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources tie Medicaid pharmacy coverage to the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs / List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. "Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] and "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources recognize that some drugs are payable through Medicaid despite Medicare-related billing rules. "may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023] and "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual gives the specific Medicare Part D rule, while the policy excerpts do not. "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] versus the policy excerpt's broader discussion of practitioner-administered drugs and billing through Medical Benefit or Pharmacy Benefit [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023].

The provider manual expressly limits Medicaid payment to drugs whose class is "specifically excluded by law" from Part D, while the policy excerpts do not state that Part D exclusion standard. "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] and the policy excerpt only says certain practitioner-administered drugs "may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023].

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_19.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_19.pm_ground_truth: Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_19.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_20.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid may pay for certain Medicare Part D-excluded drugs only when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D, while the newest policy source dated 2023-03-01 says the same basic rule in NYRx transition guidance.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage: “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01]. The older policy source is consistent in recognizing that some drugs may be billed through both benefits, but it does not add a different Part D-exclusion rule in the excerpt provided: “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — 2019-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states: “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01]. It then limits Medicaid payment to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list when the drug class is “specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans,” including “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources limit Medicaid payment to drugs that are specifically excluded from Medicare Part D by law, rather than broadly covering all Part D-member prescriptions: “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01]; “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources give the same examples of excluded drug types in the excerpts: “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is more explicit and restrictive in operational terms, stating: “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy excerpt states the payment rule but does not use that same blanket billing prohibition language in the provided text [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01].

The policy excerpt is framed as NYRx transition guidance, while the provider manual excerpt is current policy guidance and is newer: 2023-03-01 versus 2025-10-01 [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01]. Because the provider manual is newer, it should guide current handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs by default.
comparison: Both sources limit Medicaid payment to drugs that are specifically excluded from Medicare Part D by law, rather than broadly covering all Part D-member prescriptions: “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01]; “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources give the same examples of excluded drug types in the excerpts: “Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is more explicit and restrictive in operational terms, stating: “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy excerpt states the payment rule but does not use that same blanket billing prohibition language in the provided text [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01].

The policy excerpt is framed as NYRx transition guidance, while the provider manual excerpt is current policy guidance and is newer: 2023-03-01 versus 2025-10-01 [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — 2023-03-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — 2025-10-01]. Because the provider manual is newer, it should guide current handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs by default.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Because the provider manual is newer, it should guide current handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs by default."]}
test_id_20.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

test_id_21.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacy manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 and the provider manual both require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual adds a specific Medicare-first rule for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] An older 2022 Medicaid Update says the same general rule: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort” and “all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2 — 2022-04-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source is more specific for Medicare: “Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at: https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx. Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01] The manual also states that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources require Medicaid to be billed after other liable coverage/resources are used: policy says providers must bill “applicable third parties” before Medicaid and that Medicaid is “the payer of last resort,” while the manual says “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources place Medicaid in a secondary payer role rather than first payer: policy says “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment,” and the manual says “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The policy source states the general coordination-of-benefits rule for all liable third parties, but it does not specifically say that Medicare must be billed first for dual-covered pharmacy services. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]

The provider manual is more specific and current on Medicare billing: it says pharmacies “are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program” and that “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]
comparison: Both sources require Medicaid to be billed after other liable coverage/resources are used: policy says providers must bill “applicable third parties” before Medicaid and that Medicaid is “the payer of last resort,” while the manual says “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources place Medicaid in a secondary payer role rather than first payer: policy says “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment,” and the manual says “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]

The policy source states the general coordination-of-benefits rule for all liable third parties, but it does not specifically say that Medicare must be billed first for dual-covered pharmacy services. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — 2025-04-01]

The provider manual is more specific and current on Medicare billing: it says pharmacies “are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program” and that “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

• Provider agencies and practitioners must adhere to all Medicaid requirements in
this Manual and in other applicable provider manuals, regulations, and statutes.
• Provider agencies must adhere to cultural competency guidelines
• Provider agencies must be knowledgeable and have experience in trauma-
informed care and working with individuals from the cultural groups of those
being served
• The provider agency must ensure that staff receive Mandated Reporting training
prior to service delivery
VERSION 2023-1 MARCH 2023 PAGE | 73

Children’s Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Manual

applicable

state agency is obtained and maintained
• The provider agency must ensure that any safety precautions needed to protect
the child/youth population served are taken as necessary and required by the
designating State agency
Additional information and application for Children’s HCBS can be found on the DOH
website.

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

test_id_21.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_21.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_21.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_22.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_22.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default for this issue. Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual adds a specific Medicare-first rule for covered services: "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 says NYS Medicaid providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]. The earlier policy update says the same general rule: providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01 states that pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid"; it further specifies that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The same page also says the Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that the Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and the manual says Medicare must be maximized before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy update states only the general third-party-liability rule and does not specifically mention Medicare-first billing for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific and current: it expressly says pharmacies must participate in Medicare and that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and the manual says Medicare must be maximized before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy update states only the general third-party-liability rule and does not specifically mention Medicare-first billing for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific and current: it expressly says pharmacies must participate in Medicare and that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_22.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default for this issue. Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual adds a specific Medicare-first rule for covered services: \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 says NYS Medicaid providers \"are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" and that \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]. The earlier policy update says the same general rule: providers \"are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01 states that pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers \"are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program\" and that \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid\"; it further specifies that \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The same page also says the Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that the Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, \"must be accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: \"bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and the manual says Medicare must be maximized before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy update states only the general third-party-liability rule and does not specifically mention Medicare-first billing for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].", "The provider manual is more specific and current: it expressly says pharmacies must participate in Medicare and that \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."]}
test_id_22.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_22.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_22.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.
