answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, pharmacies must maximize Medicare benefits before billing Medicaid, and this source is more up to date overall than the newest policy source dated 2025-04-01, so it should guide current practice by default. The policy source says NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that applicable third parties must be billed before Medicaid, which is consistent with the manual’s Medicare-first rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] An older policy reminder says the same general rule: "Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits" and providers must bill third parties before Medicaid because "Medicaid is always the payor of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance is more specific to pharmacy and Medicare: "Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement" and "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also states that pharmacies enrolled as billing providers must demonstrate participation in Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require Medicaid to be billed only after other applicable coverage is used: policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before Medicaid and that Medicaid is "the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], while the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources support a coordination-of-benefits / last-resort framework rather than allowing Medicaid to be billed first: policy says "all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], and the manual says services covered by both programs "must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy source is broader and does not single out Medicare; it speaks generally about "applicable third parties" and "all other available resources" before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], while the provider manual specifically says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized" and that dual-covered services "must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual adds pharmacy-specific enrollment and payment rules not stated in the policy excerpt, including that pharmacies "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that Medicare/Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17-18 — Oct 01, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require Medicaid to be billed only after other applicable coverage is used: policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before Medicaid and that Medicaid is "the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], while the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources support a coordination-of-benefits / last-resort framework rather than allowing Medicaid to be billed first: policy says "all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], and the manual says services covered by both programs "must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy source is broader and does not single out Medicare; it speaks generally about "applicable third parties" and "all other available resources" before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], while the provider manual specifically says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized" and that dual-covered services "must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual adds pharmacy-specific enrollment and payment rules not stated in the policy excerpt, including that pharmacies "are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and that Medicare/Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17-18 — Oct 01, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require Medicaid to be billed only after other applicable coverage is used: policy says providers must bill \"applicable third parties\" before Medicaid and that Medicaid is \"the payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], while the manual says \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources support a coordination-of-benefits / last-resort framework rather than allowing Medicaid to be billed first: policy says \"all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], and the manual says services covered by both programs \"must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy source is broader and does not single out Medicare; it speaks generally about \"applicable third parties\" and \"all other available resources\" before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025], while the provider manual specifically says \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized\" and that dual-covered services \"must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The provider manual adds pharmacy-specific enrollment and payment rules not stated in the policy excerpt, including that pharmacies \"are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program\" and that Medicare/Medicaid payment \"must be accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17-18 — Oct 01, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."]}
test_id_23.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.

Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_24.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_24.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, pharmacies must maximize Medicare before billing Medicaid, and this source is more up to date overall than the newest policy source dated 2023-03-01, so it should receive more weight by default. The policy excerpt is older and only says pharmacies must bill the appropriate party, including Medicare or Medicaid, without stating the Medicare-before-Medicaid rule as directly. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.policy_definition: The policy excerpts do not give a direct Medicare-before-Medicaid rule for pharmacies; the closest guidance says, “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] The newer policy manual excerpt is general and says providers must “adhere to all Medicaid requirements in this Manual and in other applicable provider manuals, regulations, and statutes,” but it does not itself spell out the pharmacy billing order. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:100 — Mar 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states the rule directly: “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says pharmacies enrolled as billing providers “are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require pharmacies/providers to direct claims to the appropriate payer rather than billing Medicaid first by default: the policy says bill “to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid,” and the provider manual says Medicare must be maximized before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place Medicaid billing within a broader set of Medicaid requirements and payer coordination rules rather than treating Medicaid as the only payer. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:100 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is explicit that “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare,” while the policy excerpt only says the pharmacy must bill “the appropriate party” and names Medicare and Medicaid as possible payers without stating the order. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]

The provider manual adds a Medicare participation requirement for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies: “Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program,” which is not stated in the policy excerpts provided. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require pharmacies/providers to direct claims to the appropriate payer rather than billing Medicaid first by default: the policy says bill “to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid,” and the provider manual says Medicare must be maximized before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place Medicaid billing within a broader set of Medicaid requirements and payer coordination rules rather than treating Medicaid as the only payer. [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:100 — Mar 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is explicit that “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare,” while the policy excerpt only says the pharmacy must bill “the appropriate party” and names Medicare and Medicaid as possible payers without stating the order. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]

The provider manual adds a Medicare participation requirement for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacies: “Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program,” which is not stated in the policy excerpts provided. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
• Provider agencies and practitioners adhere to all Medicaid requirements in
this Manual and in other applicable provider manuals, regulations, and
statutes
• Provider agencies adhere to cultural competency guidelines
• Provider agencies must be knowledgeable and have experience in trauma-
informed care and working with individuals from the cultural groups of those
being served
• The provider agency ensures that staff receive Mandated Reporting training
prior to service delivery
• The provider agency ensures that practitioners maintain the licensure
necessary to provide services under their scope of practice under State law if
applicable
• The provider agency ensures that any insurance required by the designating
State agency is obtained and maintained
• The provider agency ensures that any safety precautions needed to protect
the child population served are taken as necessary and required by the
designating State agency
Additional information and application for Children’s HCBS can be found on the DOH
website.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

test_id_25.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_25.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacy manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 and the provider manual both require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual adds a specific Medicare rule that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] An older policy reminder says the same general rule: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort” and “all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source is more specific for pharmacy billing: “Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program” and “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states that “The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient” and that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other available coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: “bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and the pharmacy manual requires Medicare to be billed first for dual-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy source states a broad third-party-liability rule for all NYS Medicaid providers, but it does not specifically say that Medicare must be billed first for pharmacy claims: “bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific for pharmacies and Medicare: “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare” and pharmacies “are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other available coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: “bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and the pharmacy manual requires Medicare to be billed first for dual-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The policy source states a broad third-party-liability rule for all NYS Medicaid providers, but it does not specifically say that Medicare must be billed first for pharmacy claims: “bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific for pharmacies and Medicare: “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare” and pharmacies “are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• Provider agencies and practitioners must adhere to all Medicaid requirements in
this Manual and in other applicable provider manuals, regulations, and statutes.
• Provider agencies must adhere to cultural competency guidelines
• Provider agencies must be knowledgeable and have experience in trauma-
informed care and working with individuals from the cultural groups of those
being served
• The provider agency must ensure that staff receive Mandated Reporting training
prior to service delivery
VERSION 2023-1 MARCH 2023 PAGE | 73

Children’s Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Manual
• The provider agency must ensure that practitioners maintain the licensure
necessary to provide services under their scope of practice under State law if
applicable
• The provider agency must ensure that any insurance required by the designating
state agency is obtained and maintained
• The provider agency must ensure that any safety precautions needed to protect
the child/youth population served are taken as necessary and required by the
designating State agency
Additional information and application for Children’s HCBS can be found on the DOH
website.

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_25.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacy manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 and the provider manual both require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual adds a specific Medicare rule that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] An older policy reminder says the same general rule: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort” and “all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2022]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual source is more specific for pharmacy billing: “Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program” and “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states that “The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient” and that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require other available coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: “bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and the pharmacy manual requires Medicare to be billed first for dual-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy source states a broad third-party-liability rule for all NYS Medicaid providers, but it does not specifically say that Medicare must be billed first for pharmacy claims: “bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].", "The provider manual is more specific for pharmacies and Medicare: “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare” and pharmacies “are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."]}
test_id_25.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_25.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_25.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_26.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_26.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, Medicaid requires providers to pursue available other coverage before Medicaid payment, and failure to obtain prior authorization does not override that responsibility. The newest policy source dated 2022-03-01 is older, so the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on crossover claims and prior approval after Medicaid payment. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update March 2022 Volume 38 Number 3:7 — Mar 1, 2022]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says crossover claims are only authorized for certain Medicare crossover billing: “HCPCS codes ‘G2082’ and ‘G2083’ should not be billed to the NYS Medicaid program. These codes are a bundled payment and are not eligible for rebate collection per Federal rule and, therefore, are not listed as covered by the program. These codes are only authorized for use on Medicare crossover claims.” [New York State Medicaid Update March 2022 Volume 38 Number 3:7 — Mar 1, 2022] Earlier policy guidance also says third-party issues, including prior authorization, must be resolved before Medicaid submission: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 1, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that Medicaid is secondary to other coverage and that prior authorization problems do not excuse that order: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit...” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025] This means the provider manual requires other coverage to be pursued first, and it does not treat missing prior authorization as a basis to bypass that requirement. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require third-party/other coverage to be addressed before Medicaid payment: “providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 1, 2020] and “a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].

Both sources tie prior authorization to the third-party billing process rather than allowing Medicaid to pay first: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 1, 2020] and “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit...” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and gives the clearest current rule, stating that other coverage must be pursued first and that missing prior authorization does not override that rule: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” and “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit...” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].

The policy excerpts are older and more limited on crossover claims; the newest policy chunk only says certain HCPCS codes “are only authorized for use on Medicare crossover claims,” while the older policy chunk separately says third-party PA issues must be resolved before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update March 2022 Volume 38 Number 3:7 — Mar 1, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 1, 2020].
comparison: Both sources require third-party/other coverage to be addressed before Medicaid payment: “providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 1, 2020] and “a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].

Both sources tie prior authorization to the third-party billing process rather than allowing Medicaid to pay first: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 1, 2020] and “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit...” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and gives the clearest current rule, stating that other coverage must be pursued first and that missing prior authorization does not override that rule: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” and “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit...” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].

The policy excerpts are older and more limited on crossover claims; the newest policy chunk only says certain HCPCS codes “are only authorized for use on Medicare crossover claims,” while the older policy chunk separately says third-party PA issues must be resolved before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update March 2022 Volume 38 Number 3:7 — Mar 1, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 1, 2020].

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: HCPCS codes “G2082” and “G2083” should not be billed to the NYS Medicaid program.
These codes are a bundled payment and are not eligible for rebate collection per Federal rule and,
therefore, are not listed as covered by the program. These codes are only authorized for use on
Medicare crossover claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update March 2022 Volume 38 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
July 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 8

Updated Pharmacy Guidance for Long Term Care Providers and
Prior Authorization Requirements
Effective June 25, 2020, system changes have been made to allow for providers to initiate a bypass from
prior authorization (PA) when:
 a member is a resident of a Long Term Care (LTC) facility which is either a Private Skilled Nursing
Facility, Public Skilled Nursing Facility, Private Health Related Facility, or Public Health Related
Facility (identified as “NH” on an eligibility response), and
 a member first obtains Medicaid eligibility after 90 days from the prescription date of service/fill date
for claims not included in the rate. Please see in this Medicaid Update, article titled Pharmacy Fee-
For-Service Billing Guidance: Long Term Care and Foster Care (Child Care) Facilities for the full
guidance for LTC facilities.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions

00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_26.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:

To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.

policy.text:
Please note: HCPCS codes “G2082” and “G2083” should not be billed to the NYS Medicaid program.
These codes are a bundled payment and are not eligible for rebate collection per Federal rule and,
therefore, are not listed as covered by the program. These codes are only authorized for use on
Medicare crossover claims.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the benefit transition, that require pre-approval. To ensure
a smooth transition for MMC enrollees, DMEPOS authorization requirements will be temporarily
relaxed (e.g., claims will not deny) during the transition period which starts April 1, 2023.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions
Code
Item Not Eligible For Product not on
00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.

test_id_28.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_28.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_28.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_29.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
