answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, Medicaid remains secondary to other coverage and any prior authorization issues must be resolved before the claim is submitted; this newer provider manual should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2020-10-01 says the same basic rule for crossover/third-party claims: bill the other payer first and resolve any PA requirements before billing Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says that for claims where Medicaid is secondary, providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting to Medicaid, and that the claim must include the other payer’s Explanation of Benefits when required: “providers must first bill Medicare or the other third- party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” and “providers will be required to attach an Explanation of Benefits” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020]. The earlier policy excerpt states the same crossover rule more explicitly for PA: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid” and “If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says Medicaid is payer of last resort and other coverage must be used first: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” and “a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also says that if there is a responsible third party, “that responsible third party must pay first,” and that “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit…” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid: “providers must first bill Medicare or the other third- party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and “a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources require prior authorization issues to be addressed before Medicaid billing: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit…” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and frames the rule as a general federal/Medicaid last-resort requirement: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts focus more narrowly on billing Medicare/other third-party insurance first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020].

The policy excerpts specifically mention an Explanation of Benefits attachment requirement: “providers will be required to attach an Explanation of Benefits” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020], while the provider manual excerpt provided here does not mention EOB attachment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid: “providers must first bill Medicare or the other third- party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and “a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources require prior authorization issues to be addressed before Medicaid billing: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit…” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and frames the rule as a general federal/Medicaid last-resort requirement: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts focus more narrowly on billing Medicare/other third-party insurance first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020].

The policy excerpts specifically mention an Explanation of Benefits attachment requirement: “providers will be required to attach an Explanation of Benefits” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020], while the provider manual excerpt provided here does not mention EOB attachment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.

policy.text:
Prior authorization (PA) is required to
obtain the following medication beyond the following limits:
DOSE OPTIMIZATION CHART
CARDIOVASCULAR AGENTS
Antiarrhythmics
Drug Name, Strength Dose Optimization Limitations
In case of dose titration for these medications, the
Department will allow for multi-day dosing (up to 2
amiodarone 100mg 1 daily Tablet doses/daily) for loading dose for 30 days
CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM AGENTS
Anticonvulsants
Drug Name, Strength Dose Optimization Limitations
Aptiom® 200mg, 400mg 1 daily Tablet
Fycompa® 4mg, 6mg 1 daily Tablet
topiramate ER 100mg 1 daily Capsule
In case of dose titration for these medications, the
Department will allow for multi-day dosing (up to 2
Lamictal XR® 50mg 1 daily Tablet doses/daily) for titration purposes for 90 days
In case of dose titration for these medications, the
Department will allow for multi-day dosing (up to 2
Oxtellar XR® 300mg 1 daily Tablet doses/daily) for titration purposes for 90 days
Central Nervous System (CNS) Stimulants
Drug Name, Strength Dose Optimization Limitations
Vyvanse® 10mg, 40mg 1 daily Capsule
GASTROINTESTINAL AGENTS
Proton Pump Inhibitors
Drug Name, Strength Dose Optimization Limitations
Nexium® 10mg, 20mg 1 daily Packet
HEMATOLOGIC AGENTS
Anticoagulants – Oral
Drug Name, Strength Dose Optimization Limitations
Xarelto® 10mg 1 daily Tablet
PA requirements are not dependent on the date a prescription is written.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).

It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, providers must pursue other coverage first and "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. The newest policy source dated 2021-03-01 is older, so the provider manual should guide current handling of crossover claims unless you are asking about historical policy [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01].
answers.policy_definition: The policy excerpts say that for Medicaid as payor of last resort, providers must bill Medicare or other third-party insurance first, and "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. The later policy excerpt also says providers "must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" and that an Explanation of Benefits will be required to be attached to Medicaid claims [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]. The policy excerpts do not separately define a post-Medicaid-payment prior approval process; they focus on resolving third-party PA requirements before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt states that Medicaid requires use of all available resources before payment and that a provider must pursue other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid; if another party is the primary payor, "that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. It also says, "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. The excerpt does not spell out a separate after-payment prior approval rule for crossover claims; it instead emphasizes prior pursuit of other coverage and prior authorization before Medicaid billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources require third-party coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources tie Medicaid billing to prior authorization/claim issues with other coverage: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01] and "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy excerpts explicitly mention resolving third-party PA requirements before Medicaid submission, but they do not state a separate rule about prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

The provider manual is newer and more current, and it frames the rule as a continuing obligation to use other coverage first and that failure to obtain prior authorization does not excuse that responsibility, rather than describing a post-payment prior approval requirement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:

may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.

Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

test_id_31.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the current guidance is that all available resources must be used before Medicaid pays and a responsible third party must pay first; this provider manual is more up to date than the newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 and should receive more weight by default. The policy and manual are aligned on COB as payer-of-last-resort billing, but the provider manual adds more operational detail for current pharmacy billing. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort"; it also states that "Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance similarly says "Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] The policy excerpts also require that the COB claim "accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB)." [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] It further states that "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also gives a pharmacy-specific example: for a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: policy says providers must "bill applicable third parties" and that "all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment," while the manual says "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and providers must pursue "any available other coverage" first. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort / payor of last resort: policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" and earlier policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort," while the manual requires the responsible third party to pay first before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more operational and current for pharmacy billing, adding that a provider must pursue other coverage before submitting a claim and that Workers’ Compensation must be used first for an open work-related injury case; the policy excerpts do not include those pharmacy-specific instructions. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts include a claims-adjudication requirement not stated in the manual excerpts: the COB claim "must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB)." [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: policy says providers must "bill applicable third parties" and that "all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment," while the manual says "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and providers must pursue "any available other coverage" first. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more operational and current for pharmacy billing, adding that a provider must pursue other coverage before submitting a claim and that Workers’ Compensation must be used first for an open work-related injury case; the policy excerpts do not include those pharmacy-specific instructions. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts include a claims-adjudication requirement not stated in the manual excerpts: the COB claim "must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB)." policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.

Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.

pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.
o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.

test_id_31.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

test_id_32.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for COB guidance and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 is consistent on the core rule that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and third parties must be billed first. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents say NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, because Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and all other available resources must be used first. The April 2025 update states: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] The October 2022 update adds that COB “ensures the correct party pays first” and that “the responsible third-party should pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — 2022-10-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that if a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01] More generally, the manual states: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment,” and “a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid,” with the responsible third party paying first if one is primary. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage or third parties to be used before Medicaid pays: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties” and Medicaid is “the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]; “all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources place the primary payment obligation on the responsible third party first: “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — 2022-10-01]; “that responsible third party must pay first” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more specific for pharmacy claims, including Workers’ Compensation: “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts are broader and do not mention Workers’ Compensation in the COB language provided [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01].

The policy excerpts frame COB as a general Medicaid billing rule—“bill applicable third parties” and Medicaid as “payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]—whereas the provider manual adds operational claim-submission language, including that providers must “pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage or third parties to be used before Medicaid pays: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties” and Medicaid is “the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]; “all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources place the primary payment obligation on the responsible third party first: “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — 2022-10-01]; “that responsible third party must pay first” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is newer and more specific for pharmacy claims, including Workers’ Compensation: “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts are broader and do not mention Workers’ Compensation in the COB language provided [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01].

The policy excerpts frame COB as a general Medicaid billing rule—“bill applicable third parties” and Medicaid as “payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]—whereas the provider manual adds operational claim-submission language, including that providers must “pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.

of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:

www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;

Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/
health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_nov21_pr.pdf; and
• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer
ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier article published in the October 2017 issue of the Medicaid Update,
located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_33.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."
test_id_33.pm_ground_truth:
It is the pharmacy's duty to make "reasonable efforts" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission
test_id_33.policy_ground_truth: The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP "Other Payer" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

provider_manual.text:
o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.
o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.

test_id_34.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

test_id_35.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, COB requires using other available coverage first before billing Medicaid, and the provider manual should receive more weight by default because it is newer than the newest policy source dated 2025-04-01. Both sources say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that other liable third parties must pay first. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] The older policy guidance similarly states that "Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first," that "NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort," and that "the responsible third-party should pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — 2022-10-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." It also says that if there is a responsible third party who should be paying as primary payor, "that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01] For workers' compensation, the manual adds that if a member has an open work-related injury case, "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer/payor of last resort" and other available resources must be used first. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources require billing or using liable third parties before Medicaid: "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual is newer and more detailed on process, stating that providers must "pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid," while the policy excerpt focuses on billing third parties before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]

The provider manual includes a workers' compensation-specific rule: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program," which is not stated in the newest policy excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]
comparison: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer/payor of last resort" and other available resources must be used first. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources require billing or using liable third parties before Medicaid: "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

The provider manual is newer and more detailed on process, stating that providers must "pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid," while the policy excerpt focuses on billing third parties before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]

The provider manual includes a workers' compensation-specific rule: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program," which is not stated in the newest policy excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — 2025-04-01]

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits article published in the April 2022 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/
health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_nov21_pr.pdf; and
• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer
ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier article published in the October 2017 issue of the Medicaid Update,
located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.
o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.
