test_id_36.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist is not required to enter missing member address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 instead says that when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber, so the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define a general rule for missing patient or prescriber information; it only states that for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, "the pharmacist must notify the prescr..." when the prescription is presented [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023]. The older policy excerpts likewise do not address missing patient or prescriber information directly; they discuss supervising pharmacist duties and pharmacy responsibility generally, such as "The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive role" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023] and "As enrolled providers, pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists have a corresponding responsibility to ensure" compliance with pharmacy standards [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — Apr 01, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that if "the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription" when the information "Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or" when "The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025]. This excerpt addresses missing member-identifying information, not prescriber information, and it gives a conditional exception rather than a universal duty to complete the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist in the prescription process, but the policy excerpt frames it as notifying the prescriber for PA-related non-preferred drugs while the manual frames it as deciding whether missing member information must be entered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Neither source excerpt provides a broad, all-purpose rule covering every kind of missing patient or prescriber information; each excerpt is limited to a specific context [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives an explicit exception for missing member address, age, or CIN: "the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items" if the stated conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt does not provide a comparable exception for missing patient information [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].

The policy excerpt focuses on a prescriber-facing action—"the pharmacist must notify the prescr..." when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023]—whereas the provider manual focuses on whether the pharmacist must enter missing member data on the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist in the prescription process, but the policy excerpt frames it as notifying the prescriber for PA-related non-preferred drugs while the manual frames it as deciding whether missing member information must be entered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Neither source excerpt provides a broad, all-purpose rule covering every kind of missing patient or prescriber information; each excerpt is limited to a specific context [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual gives an explicit exception for missing member address, age, or CIN: "the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items" if the stated conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt does not provide a comparable exception for missing patient information [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].

The policy excerpt focuses on a prescriber-facing action—"the pharmacist must notify the prescr..." when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023]—whereas the provider manual focuses on whether the pharmacist must enter missing member data on the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible to replace lost, stolen, or mis-directed drugs at no additional cost to the
member or the Medicaid Program.
• Pharmacy providers who deliver medication without member or authorized individual consent will be
required to accept the return of the medication, credit Medicaid the claim, and destroy those drugs per
State Law.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs not picked up or delivered must be reversed within 60 days or sooner as
required by the mandatory part of New York State’s compliance programs under 18 NYCRR §521.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2019 Volume 35 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible for preparing and delivering the drug in accordance with administration
guidelines in the package insert, as well as the replacement of improperly stored, lost, or stolen drugs until
confirmed receipt by the authorized agent.
• The pharmacy must confirm an appropriate administration plan is in place for home deliveries.
• The pharmacy is required to obtain documentation of delivery by the receipt of a signature of a recipient or
an authorized agent at the site of administration.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs that were not deliverable must be reversed within 14 days.
• Once delivered and signed for, the site of administration is responsible for replacement of improperly
stored, handled, lost, or stolen practitioner-administered drugs.
“Brown bagging” is when drugs designated for self-administration or practitioner administration are dispensed directly
to a patient by the pharmacy. Brown bagging practitioner administered drugs causes concern regarding proper
storage or handling, which can affect the drug’s efficacy. Brown bagging is acceptable only when the drug is intended,
prescribed, or labeled for self-administration.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_36.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist is not required to enter missing member address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 instead says that when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber, so the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt does not define a general rule for missing patient or prescriber information; it only states that for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, \"the pharmacist must notify the prescr...\" when the prescription is presented [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023]. The older policy excerpts likewise do not address missing patient or prescriber information directly; they discuss supervising pharmacist duties and pharmacy responsibility generally, such as \"The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive role\" [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023] and \"As enrolled providers, pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists have a corresponding responsibility to ensure\" compliance with pharmacy standards [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — Apr 01, 2023].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says that if \"the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription\" when the information \"Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or\" when \"The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025]. This excerpt addresses missing member-identifying information, not prescriber information, and it gives a conditional exception rather than a universal duty to complete the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist in the prescription process, but the policy excerpt frames it as notifying the prescriber for PA-related non-preferred drugs while the manual frames it as deciding whether missing member information must be entered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Neither source excerpt provides a broad, all-purpose rule covering every kind of missing patient or prescriber information; each excerpt is limited to a specific context [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual gives an explicit exception for missing member address, age, or CIN: \"the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items\" if the stated conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt does not provide a comparable exception for missing patient information [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].", "The policy excerpt focuses on a prescriber-facing action—\"the pharmacist must notify the prescr...\" when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023]—whereas the provider manual focuses on whether the pharmacist must enter missing member data on the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025]."]}
test_id_36.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

test_id_37.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_37.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, pharmacists are not required to enter missing member address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 is older and only addresses a related prior-authorization workflow, so the provider manual should guide current practice for missing required information.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define a general pharmacist duty for missing patient or prescriber information; it only says that for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, “When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]. A separate policy excerpt says pharmacists may work with the patient and contact the prescriber to change the prescription or obtain PA after a transition fill, but it does not address missing required information on the prescription itself [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition:4-5 — 2023-01-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that if “the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription” when the information “is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled,” or when “The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01]. The excerpt also notes that controlled substances must still meet Article 33 requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources place the pharmacist in an active role when prescription-related information is incomplete or needs follow-up, rather than treating the issue as purely administrative [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources preserve pharmacist judgment/interaction with the prescriber or legitimacy review: the policy excerpt says the pharmacist must notify the prescriber in the PA context, and the manual says the pharmacist need not enter missing items if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct rule for missing member information—address, age, or CIN—and says the pharmacist is “not required to enter” those items under stated conditions, while the policy excerpt does not provide that specific missing-information rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].

The policy excerpt focuses on prior authorization for non-preferred drugs and says the pharmacist must notify the prescriber, whereas the provider manual focuses on whether missing patient identifiers must be entered and on prescription legitimacy [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources place the pharmacist in an active role when prescription-related information is incomplete or needs follow-up, rather than treating the issue as purely administrative [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources preserve pharmacist judgment/interaction with the prescriber or legitimacy review: the policy excerpt says the pharmacist must notify the prescriber in the PA context, and the manual says the pharmacist need not enter missing items if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual gives a direct rule for missing member information—address, age, or CIN—and says the pharmacist is “not required to enter” those items under stated conditions, while the policy excerpt does not provide that specific missing-information rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].

The policy excerpt focuses on prior authorization for non-preferred drugs and says the pharmacist must notify the prescriber, whereas the provider manual focuses on whether missing patient identifiers must be entered and on prescription legitimacy [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — 2025-10-01].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Record Retention, Furnishing of Records, and Permitting Audit and Cooperation
with Governmental Agencies
As New York State (NYS) Medicaid-enrolled providers, in accordance with Title 18 of the New York Codes,
Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) §504.3(a) and §504.3(g), located at: https://regs.health.ny.gov/content/
section-5043-duties-provider, the pharmacy owner and the supervising pharmacist are responsible for:
• preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records demonstrating the right to receive payment
under the medical assistance program [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
• keeping for a period of six years from the date the care, services or supplies were furnished, all records
necessary to disclose the nature and extent of services furnished and all information regarding claims
for payment submitted by, or on behalf of, the provider [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
Please note: Providers continue to be subject to any additional record retention requirements
outlined in their prior or current contracts with Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) or Managed
Long Term Care (MLTC) Plans or Subcontractors thereof, including retention for 10 years or
more as delineated therein.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_38.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

test_id_39.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist is not required to enter missing member address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 is older and only says the pharmacist must notify the prescriber when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization, so the provider manual should guide current practice for missing required information. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4-5 — 2025-10-01] [mu_no12_jul23_pr.pdf:11 — 2023-07-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define a pharmacist duty for missing patient or prescriber information generally; it only states that when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization, “the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” and that PA requests “must be submitted by the prescriber or by an authorized agent of the prescriber.” [mu_no12_jul23_pr.pdf:11 — 2023-07-01] The other policy excerpts address different responsibilities, such as supervising pharmacists’ general duties and transition-fill follow-up, but they do not specify what to do when required patient or prescriber information is missing. [mu_no10_may23_pr.pdf:9 — 2023-05-01] [mu_no8_apr23_pr.pdf:12 — 2023-04-01] [mu_no1_jan23_speced_pr.pdf:4-5 — 2023-01-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says that if “the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing,” “the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription” if the information “is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled,” or if “the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4-5 — 2025-10-01] The excerpt does not add a separate rule for missing prescriber information, but it does state that “The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:27-28 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist in relation to prescription validity and prescriber involvement, rather than treating the issue as purely administrative. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:27-28 — 2025-10-01] [mu_no12_jul23_pr.pdf:11 — 2023-07-01]

Both sources show that the pharmacist may need to act when information is incomplete, either by verifying legitimacy/identity or by notifying the prescriber in the PA context. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4-5 — 2025-10-01] [mu_no12_jul23_pr.pdf:11 — 2023-07-01]
differences: The provider manual gives a direct rule for missing member information: the pharmacist is “not required to enter” missing address, age, or CIN if specified conditions are met. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4-5 — 2025-10-01] The policy excerpt does not provide that kind of missing-information rule. [mu_no12_jul23_pr.pdf:11 — 2023-07-01]

The policy excerpt focuses on prior authorization workflow for non-preferred drugs, stating that PA requests “must be submitted by the prescriber or by an authorized agent” and that the pharmacist must notify the prescriber. [mu_no12_jul23_pr.pdf:11 — 2023-07-01] The provider manual instead focuses on whether the pharmacist must enter missing member data and on a good-faith effort to verify prescription validity and prescriber identity. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4-5 — 2025-10-01]

The provider manual gives a direct rule for missing member information: the pharmacist is “not required to enter” missing address, age, or CIN if specified conditions are met. authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the

the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.

effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.

test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist is not required to enter missing member address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 instead says that when a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber, so the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define a general rule for missing patient or prescriber information; it only states that for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, “the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” when the prescription is presented [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023]. The other policy excerpts address unrelated supervising-pharmacist or pharmacy responsibilities and do not specify what to do when required patient or prescriber information is missing [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:9 — May 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:12 — Apr 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Update July 2019 Volume 35 Number 8:3 — Jul 01, 2019].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says: “If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription if the information: • Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or • The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025]. This excerpt is limited to missing member address, age, or CIN and does not address missing prescriber information [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist in the prescription process rather than on the member alone, but the policy excerpt is about notifying the prescriber for PA and the manual excerpt is about whether missing member data must be entered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources show that the pharmacist’s action depends on the circumstances of the prescription: the policy requires notification when a non-preferred drug needs PA, while the manual allows omission of certain missing member fields when the pharmacist is satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific exception for missing member address, age, or CIN and says the pharmacist is “not required to enter any of these items” if the stated conditions are met, while the policy excerpt does not provide a comparable missing-information exception [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].

The policy excerpt focuses on a different duty—“the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” for prior authorization of non-preferred drugs—so it addresses prescriber notification rather than handling missing patient identifiers [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].
comparison: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist in the prescription process rather than on the member alone, but the policy excerpt is about notifying the prescriber for PA and the manual excerpt is about whether missing member data must be entered [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources show that the pharmacist’s action depends on the circumstances of the prescription: the policy requires notification when a non-preferred drug needs PA, while the manual allows omission of certain missing member fields when the pharmacist is satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual gives a specific exception for missing member address, age, or CIN and says the pharmacist is “not required to enter any of these items” if the stated conditions are met, while the policy excerpt does not provide a comparable missing-information exception [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].

The policy excerpt focuses on a different duty—“the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” for prior authorization of non-preferred drugs—so it addresses prescriber notification rather than handling missing patient identifiers [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible to replace lost, stolen, or mis-directed drugs at no additional cost to the
member or the Medicaid Program.
• Pharmacy providers who deliver medication without member or authorized individual consent will be
required to accept the return of the medication, credit Medicaid the claim, and destroy those drugs per
State Law.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs not picked up or delivered must be reversed within 60 days or sooner as
required by the mandatory part of New York State’s compliance programs under 18 NYCRR §521.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2019 Volume 35 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible for preparing and delivering the drug in accordance with administration
guidelines in the package insert, as well as the replacement of improperly stored, lost, or stolen drugs until
confirmed receipt by the authorized agent.
• The pharmacy must confirm an appropriate administration plan is in place for home deliveries.
• The pharmacy is required to obtain documentation of delivery by the receipt of a signature of a recipient or
an authorized agent at the site of administration.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs that were not deliverable must be reversed within 14 days.
• Once delivered and signed for, the site of administration is responsible for replacement of improperly
stored, handled, lost, or stolen practitioner-administered drugs.
“Brown bagging” is when drugs designated for self-administration or practitioner administration are dispensed directly
to a patient by the pharmacy. Brown bagging practitioner administered drugs causes concern regarding proper
storage or handling, which can affect the drug’s efficacy. Brown bagging is acceptable only when the drug is intended,
prescribed, or labeled for self-administration.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_40.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."
test_id_40.pm_ground_truth: A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.
test_id_40.policy_ground_truth: The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice.

test_id_41.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources indicate that drugs used for weight loss are not covered/excluded, so the answer is yes, weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]; [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says that "Wegovy 0.25 mg/0.5 ml pen" has a "State Plan exclusion" and that "Indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2)" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]. Earlier policy guidance also states that exclusions include "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt does not name weight loss drugs specifically, but it states that Medicaid pays only for drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs and that "drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. It also says that "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources treat weight-loss-related drug use as not covered/excluded: policy says "Indications for weight loss are not covered" and the manual says Medicaid only pays for drugs that are not excluded by law and that compounds may not be made for "NYS Medicaid excluded indications" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources frame the restriction as an exclusion rather than a covered benefit: policy uses "State Plan exclusion" and the manual limits payment to drugs on the reimbursable list and excludes drugs/indications that are not allowed [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy source is more explicit about the concept itself, stating that "Indications for weight loss are not covered" and naming Wegovy as a "State Plan exclusion" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01].

The provider manual is more general and does not specifically mention weight loss drugs in the provided excerpts; it instead describes broader reimbursement rules and excluded drugs/indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources treat weight-loss-related drug use as not covered/excluded: policy says "Indications for weight loss are not covered" and the manual says Medicaid only pays for drugs that are not excluded by law and that compounds may not be made for "NYS Medicaid excluded indications" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources frame the restriction as an exclusion rather than a covered benefit: policy uses "State Plan exclusion" and the manual limits payment to drugs on the reimbursable list and excludes drugs/indications that are not allowed [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01].

The policy source is more explicit about the concept itself, stating that "Indications for weight loss are not covered" and naming Wegovy as a "State Plan exclusion" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01].

The provider manual is more general and does not specifically mention weight loss drugs in the provided excerpts; it instead describes broader reimbursement rules and excluded drugs/indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest, encourage, or otherwise require that any practitioner
administered, or dispensed drug be billed to the Program under the pharmacy benefit. Practitioner administered drugs
will continue to be billed as a NYS Medicaid medical benefit.
Questions regarding this policy may be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy unit at (518)486-3209 or
NYRx@health.ny.gov.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
