It also says that \"Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources treat weight-loss-related drug use as not covered/excluded: policy says \"Indications for weight loss are not covered\" and the manual says Medicaid only pays for drugs that are not excluded by law and that compounds may not be made for \"NYS Medicaid excluded indications\" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources frame the restriction as an exclusion rather than a covered benefit: policy uses \"State Plan exclusion\" and the manual limits payment to drugs on the reimbursable list and excludes drugs/indications that are not allowed [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The policy source is more explicit about the concept itself, stating that \"Indications for weight loss are not covered\" and naming Wegovy as a \"State Plan exclusion\" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01].", "The provider manual is more general and does not specifically mention weight loss drugs in the provided excerpts; it instead describes broader reimbursement rules and excluded drugs/indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19; 34 — 2025-10-01]."]}
test_id_41.ground_truth:
"The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, weight-loss drugs are treated as excluded/not reimbursable when they are for “drugs used for the treatment of anorexia” and other excluded indications, so the provider manual should guide current billing decisions by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 also says “Indications for weight loss are not covered,” so both sources point to exclusion, with the provider manual being more recent. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:6 — 2025-04-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt states that “Wegovy 0.25 mg/0.5 ml pen” is a “State Plan exclusion” and that “Indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).” The policy also says NYS Medicaid “only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs,” and lists “drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid” as examples of excluded coverage. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — 2025-01-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:6 — 2025-04-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt says Medicaid “only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs” and identifies “examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid” including “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia,” which is the closest stated exclusion relevant to weight-loss-type use in the provided manual excerpts. The manual also says compounds may not contain “excluded drugs” or be made for “NYS Medicaid excluded indications,” reinforcing that excluded indications are not reimbursable. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — 2025-10-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:34 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources say Medicaid reimbursement is limited to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources describe certain drug uses/indications as not covered or not reimbursable. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:6 — 2025-04-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The policy excerpt is explicit about the target concept, stating “Indications for weight loss are not covered,” while the provider manual excerpt does not use the phrase “weight loss” and instead gives a related example: “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:6 — 2025-04-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — 2025-10-01]

The provider manual is newer and therefore should receive more weight for current guidance, but the policy source is still directly on point for weight-loss drugs and confirms exclusion. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:6 — 2025-04-01]

Both sources describe certain drug uses/indications as not covered or not reimbursable. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:6 — 2025-04-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — 2025-10-01]

The provider manual is newer and therefore should receive more weight for current guidance, but the policy source is still directly on point for weight-loss drugs and confirms exclusion. policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The excerpt provided does not specifically mention weight loss drugs or say they are excluded. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]

[Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Additional information regarding the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Program and residential health care
facilities may be accessed at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/.
See Section 4.0 for Long Term Care Pharmacy Short Cycle Billing information.
Items Provided by Child (Foster) Care Agencies
Please see this link for items provided by Childcare Agencies:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/carveout.htm.
OMH Residential Treatment Facility Prescription Drug Carve-Out
Reimbursement of prescription drugs for children and youth between the ages of five and twenty-one who are
residents of the Office of Mental Health (OMH) Residential Treatment Facilities (RTF) is a NYRx benefit and billed
directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy.
Physician administered drugs, OTC drugs, medical supplies, immunization services (vaccines and their administration),
nutritional supplies, sick room supplies, adult diapers, and durable medical equipment (DME) are not carved out of the
RTF rate and remain the responsibility of the facility.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the current guidance is that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable, and the provider manual should receive more weight by default because it is newer than the newest policy source dated 2025-01-01. The policy and provider manual are consistent on this point: both exclude weight loss drugs/agents from Medicaid coverage.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and that “Wegovy 0.25 mg/0.5 ml pen” is a “State Plan exclusion” because “Indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2)” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025]. The older policy reminder also states that exclusions include “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — Apr 01, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states that Medicaid “only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs” and that “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” are examples of drugs/drug uses “not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also says compounds “may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources say Medicaid reimbursement is limited to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list / List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources exclude weight-loss-related drugs or uses from coverage: the policy source says “Indications for weight loss are not covered,” and the policy reminder says “agents when used for the treatment of ... weight loss” are excluded [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — Apr 01, 2022].
differences: The provider manual is newer (Oct. 1, 2025) than the newest policy source (Jan. 1, 2025), so it should guide current billing/coverage questions by default [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy source is more explicit about weight-loss drugs by naming “Wegovy 0.25 mg/0.5 ml pen” and stating “Indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),” while the provider manual excerpt is broader and does not name a specific drug in the provided text [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources say Medicaid reimbursement is limited to drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list / List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources exclude weight-loss-related drugs or uses from coverage: the policy source says “Indications for weight loss are not covered,” and the policy reminder says “agents when used for the treatment of ... weight loss” are excluded [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — Apr 01, 2022].

The provider manual is newer (Oct. 1, 2025) than the newest policy source (Jan. 1, 2025), so it should guide current billing/coverage questions by default [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy source is more explicit about weight-loss drugs by naming “Wegovy 0.25 mg/0.5 ml pen” and stating “Indications for weight loss are not covered pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),” while the provider manual excerpt is broader and does not name a specific drug in the provided text [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:14 — Jan 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2022
Volume 38 | Number 4
Reminder: Some Drug Categories are Excluded from Coverage in
Pharmacy and Medical Benefits
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) programs
cover an extensive drug benefit providing access to medically necessary drugs. There are, however, certain
drug/drug classes that are not covered by Medicaid for certain diagnoses due to federal or state rules as
outlined in both the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located on the Social Security Administration “Payment
for Covered Outpatient Drugs” website, at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm and in the
eMedNY New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines document,
located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_46.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed using claim-level identifiers and, for Medicaid 340B drug claims in NCPDP format, the ingredient cost field is set to the 340B acquisition cost; this newer provider manual should guide current billing unless you are asking about older policy history. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is consistent that 340B claims must be identified correctly and that claims above the 340B ceiling price are denied and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price “will be denied” and that denied claims “must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost.” It also states that 340B-purchased drug claims “must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost,” and that claim level identifiers are required for 340B-purchased drugs billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care, or as a secondary claim. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] Older policy guidance adds that if rebates are received because of incorrect or missing claim-level identifiers, “the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the 340B discounts.” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt states that 340B covered entities must decide whether they will use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients, because federal law prohibits duplicate discounts. For Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format, the manual requires claim-level identifiers and shows the ingredient cost submitted as “340B Acquisition Cost” for Medicaid primary claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01] It also defines the 340B ceiling price as “the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage,” i.e., the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources require claim-level identification for 340B drugs and tie reimbursement to correct claim submission. The policy says 340B-purchased drug claims “must be submitted with claim level identifiers” [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], and the provider manual says the required NCPDP fields include “420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20” and “423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08” for Medicaid 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources connect reimbursement to the 340B acquisition/ceiling-price framework rather than ordinary pricing. The policy says claims must be submitted “at actual acquisition cost” and that claims above the “340B ceiling price” are denied [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], while the provider manual defines the “340B ceiling price” as AMP minus the rebate percentage (URA) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more operational: it specifies the NCPDP billing fields and shows “409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted” as “340B Acquisition Cost” for Medicaid primary claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts focus on compliance outcomes such as denial for claims above the ceiling price and resubmission with the correct ingredient cost [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01].

The policy excerpts include a rebate-recovery consequence not stated in the provider manual excerpts: if rebates are received because of incorrect or missing claim-level identifiers, “the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the 340B discounts” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01]. The provider manual excerpts instead emphasize duplicate-discount prohibition and claim formatting requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources require claim-level identification for 340B drugs and tie reimbursement to correct claim submission. The policy says 340B-purchased drug claims “must be submitted with claim level identifiers” [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01], and the provider manual says the required NCPDP fields include “420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20” and “423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08” for Medicaid 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources connect reimbursement to the 340B acquisition/ceiling-price framework rather than ordinary pricing. The policy says claims must be submitted “at actual acquisition cost” and that claims above the “340B ceiling price” are denied [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — 2023-06-01] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01], while the provider manual defines the “340B ceiling price” as AMP minus the rebate percentage (URA) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is newer and more operational: it specifies the NCPDP billing fields and shows “409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted” as “340B Acquisition Cost” for Medicaid primary claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts focus on compliance outcomes such as denial for claims above the ceiling price and resubmission with the correct ingredient cost [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — 2024-02-01].

The policy excerpts include a rebate-recovery consequence not stated in the provider manual excerpts: if rebates are received because of incorrect or missing claim-level identifiers, “the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the 340B discounts” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — 2021-11-01]. The provider manual excerpts instead emphasize duplicate-discount prohibition and claim formatting requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
4

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted * 340B Acquisition
Not applicable
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) * Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash
to Cash Customers Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_46.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_46.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_46.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 says 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, while the provider manual adds that 340B covered entities must decide whether they will use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients and that HRSA rules apply to Medicaid members. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0149 — 2025-10-01]; [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]

answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt says that upon enrollment in the 340B program, “340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for their Medicaid patients,” and that “Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts.” It also says HRSA has rules and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0149 — 2025-10-01] The manual’s definitions section also states that the “340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA).” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0175 — 2025-10-01]

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Effective September 12, 2019, for Medicaid FFS primary claims only, system editing will compare the
ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK) with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by
Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA). The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount
that a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for the purchase of a 340B covered outpatient drug.
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA
data from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid
Drug Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a
price that is used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the
URA indicates the cost of one pill.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Physician-Administered Drugs (837P/837I)
An accurate National Drug Code (NDC) and “UD” modifier must be reported for any physician-administered
drug obtained at the 340B price that are billed on the Institutional claim form for either FFS or MMC. A
physician-administered drug will not be paid under Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) if the claim does not
include an accurate NDC. Please note: Any APGs fee schedule drug will still require a provider to code the
number of units and the acquisition cost for the claim line to be paid.
Non-Drug Items
As communicated in the December 2016 issue of the Medicaid Update cited and linked in the beginning of
this article, 340B claim level identifiers are required on all 340B purchased drug claims for Medicaid members.
Pharmacies should not submit claim level identifiers on non-340B eligible items, such as test strips. Diabetic
test strips are not covered outpatient drugs and are not part of the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP),
and therefore are not eligible for a 340B discount. Only 340B purchased drugs should be tagged when claim
eligible with the 340B claim level identifiers.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
4

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted * 340B Acquisition
Not applicable
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) * Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash
to Cash Customers Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_47.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_47.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_47.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.

The newest policy source dated 2023-06-01 is consistent on identification and actual acquisition cost, but it is older background guidance. test_id_48.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_48.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_48.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_49.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B pharmacy drug claims are governed by the manual’s 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims policy and federal duplicate-discount rules, so the provider manual should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 says 340B claims are denied if the ingredient cost exceeds the 340B ceiling price and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost, while earlier policy updates require the UD modifier and proper 340B identification. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says a 340B pharmacy claim is not payable above the ceiling price: “The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] Earlier policy guidance also says 340B drugs “must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims” and that 340B drug claims are “submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider” and “properly identified as 340B” for FFS and MMC claims. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022]; [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt states that for 340B pharmacy drug claims, “Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug,” and HRSA rules apply when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual excerpt provided does not state the exact reimbursement amount or formula for 340B drugs, but it does say that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B rather than treated as ordinary drug claims: the policy says 340B drugs “must be identified by appending the UD modifier” and “properly identified as 340B,” while the manual addresses “340B Pharmacy Drug Claims” under specific 340B rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022]; [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie reimbursement to compliance with 340B-specific billing rules: the policy says claims above the 340B ceiling price “will be denied” and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost, while the manual says federal duplicate-discount rules and HRSA requirements govern when 340B drugs are used for Medicaid members. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpts give a specific reimbursement constraint for 340B drugs—claims above the 340B ceiling price “will be denied” and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost—while the provider manual excerpt provided does not give that same ceiling-price rule. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual excerpt adds federal duplicate-discount and HRSA compliance language—“manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug”—which is not stated in the policy excerpts provided. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
comparison: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B rather than treated as ordinary drug claims: the policy says 340B drugs “must be identified by appending the UD modifier” and “properly identified as 340B,” while the manual addresses “340B Pharmacy Drug Claims” under specific 340B rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022]; [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — Aug 01, 2019]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie reimbursement to compliance with 340B-specific billing rules: the policy says claims above the 340B ceiling price “will be denied” and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost, while the manual says federal duplicate-discount rules and HRSA requirements govern when 340B drugs are used for Medicaid members. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts give a specific reimbursement constraint for 340B drugs—claims above the 340B ceiling price “will be denied” and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost—while the provider manual excerpt provided does not give that same ceiling-price rule. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]; [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual excerpt adds federal duplicate-discount and HRSA compliance language—“manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug”—which is not stated in the policy excerpts provided. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
4

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted * 340B Acquisition
Not applicable
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) * Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash
to Cash Customers Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
