policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Effective September 12, 2019, for Medicaid FFS primary claims only, system editing will compare the
ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK) with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by
Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA). The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount
that a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for the purchase of a 340B covered outpatient drug.
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA
data from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid
Drug Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a
price that is used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the
URA indicates the cost of one pill.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Physician-Administered Drugs (837P/837I)
An accurate National Drug Code (NDC) and “UD” modifier must be reported for any physician-administered
drug obtained at the 340B price that are billed on the Institutional claim form for either FFS or MMC. A
physician-administered drug will not be paid under Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) if the claim does not
include an accurate NDC. Please note: Any APGs fee schedule drug will still require a provider to code the
number of units and the acquisition cost for the claim line to be paid.
Non-Drug Items
As communicated in the December 2016 issue of the Medicaid Update cited and linked in the beginning of
this article, 340B claim level identifiers are required on all 340B purchased drug claims for Medicaid members.
Pharmacies should not submit claim level identifiers on non-340B eligible items, such as test strips. Diabetic
test strips are not covered outpatient drugs and are not part of the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP),
and therefore are not eligible for a 340B discount. Only 340B purchased drugs should be tagged when claim
eligible with the 340B claim level identifiers.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_50.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B covered entities must decide whether they will use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients, and federal duplicate-discount rules apply; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 says a claim above the 340B ceiling price will be denied, while earlier policy updates require the UD modifier and NDC reporting for 340B drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says that if a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid is higher than the 340B ceiling price, “will be denied,” and that “Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] Earlier policy guidance says “Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims,” and that if the NDC is not referenced, “NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] The 2019 update also states that for Medicaid FFS primary claims, system editing compares the submitted ingredient cost with the 340B ceiling price, defined as the maximum amount a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for a 340B covered outpatient drug. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 — Aug 01, 2019]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual excerpt says that upon enrollment in the 340B program, “340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for their Medicaid patients,” and that “Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also defines the 340B ceiling price as “the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA).” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to the 340B ceiling price and limit payment when the submitted amount is too high: the policy says a claim above the ceiling price “will be denied,” and the manual defines the ceiling price as the AMP reduced by the rebate percentage/URA. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources require special 340B-related claim handling rather than ordinary billing: the policy requires the “UD” modifier and NDC reporting for 340B drugs, while the manual says covered entities must decide whether they will use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients and addresses duplicate-discount rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts focus on claim-level reimbursement mechanics—NDC reporting, the UD modifier, and denial/resubmission when the ingredient cost exceeds the 340B ceiling price—whereas the provider manual excerpt focuses on program participation and federal duplicate-discount restrictions. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]", "The provider manual is newer and therefore should guide current interpretation by default, but the policy excerpts provide more specific reimbursement processing details for 340B drug claims, including denial for claims above the ceiling price and the need to resubmit with the correct ingredient cost. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]"]}
test_id_50.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_50.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_50.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_51.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy evidence is dated 2022-07-01 and is consistent on billing responsibility, but it is older and less specific about home infusion coverage than the provider manual. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:6 — 2022-07-01]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents show that Medicaid covers certain administered drugs when they are given by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods and are for FDA-approved indications or recognized off-label indications in drug compendia: "drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. For billing responsibility, the policy says "it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" and also states that both the facility and the pharmacy must actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for patients [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]. The newer policy excerpt also notes that Medicaid may pay patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims or up to Medicaid allowed amounts for certain drugs excluded under TPL/Medicare coordination rules [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — 2021-11-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says home infusion is not paid as a bundled package: "The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Instead, "Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also says NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as drugs requiring practitioner administration and makes them available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, with pharmacy-benefit eligibility determined by criteria including that the drug "must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources place billing responsibility on the pharmacy/provider side rather than describing a bundled home-infusion payment: the policy says "it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies "must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate Medicaid coverage can apply to drugs administered by a practitioner or by injection/infusion routes: the policy covers drugs administered by "subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01], and the manual says home infusion drugs are "drugs requiring administration by a health care provider" and are available through both benefits [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current on home infusion coverage, stating there is "no... bundled payment" and that home infusion drugs and supplies "must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts only give general coverage rules for administered drugs and do not specifically define home infusion billing structure [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01].

The policy excerpts explicitly assign billing to "the pharmacy" and require billing to the correct payer such as "the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], whereas the provider manual excerpt focuses on benefit routing and eligibility criteria and does not separately state who must submit the bill [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12,19 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources place billing responsibility on the pharmacy/provider side rather than describing a bundled home-infusion payment: the policy says "it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies "must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate Medicaid coverage can apply to drugs administered by a practitioner or by injection/infusion routes: the policy covers drugs administered by "subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01], and the manual says home infusion drugs are "drugs requiring administration by a health care provider" and are available through both benefits [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is more specific and current on home infusion coverage, stating there is "no... bundled payment" and that home infusion drugs and supplies "must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts only give general coverage rules for administered drugs and do not specifically define home infusion billing structure [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01].

The policy excerpts explicitly assign billing to "the pharmacy" and require billing to the correct payer such as "the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], whereas the provider manual excerpt focuses on benefit routing and eligibility criteria and does not separately state who must submit the bill [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12,19 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_51.ground_truth:
The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_52.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_52.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs are not covered under a bundled payment; they must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit, so this source should guide current billing questions by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 is older and addresses related practitioner-administered drug coverage and billing, but it does not give the same home-infusion-specific billing rule.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says NYS Medicaid FFS covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. The older policy reminder also assigns billing responsibility to the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance is explicit for home infusion: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also states that NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as drugs requiring practitioner administration and that they may be available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit: “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources tie coverage to the benefit pathway and billing method rather than describing a single bundled home-infusion payment: the manual says home infusion drugs “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit,” and the policy materials also discuss billing to the appropriate party and benefit context. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]

Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider/pharmacy side rather than on Medicaid alone: the policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party,” and the manual says “It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23]
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current for home infusion, stating there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit,” while the policy excerpts do not give that home-infusion-specific rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]

The policy excerpts focus on practitioner-administered drugs in the practitioner’s office and on pharmacy billing to the appropriate party, whereas the provider manual specifically addresses home infusion treatments and recognizes both medical and pharmacy benefit pathways. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12, 19 — 2025-10-01]
comparison: Both sources tie coverage to the benefit pathway and billing method rather than describing a single bundled home-infusion payment: the manual says home infusion drugs “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit,” and the policy materials also discuss billing to the appropriate party and benefit context. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]

Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider/pharmacy side rather than on Medicaid alone: the policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party,” and the manual says “It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-07-23]

The provider manual is more specific and current for home infusion, stating there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit,” while the policy excerpts do not give that home-infusion-specific rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]

The policy excerpts focus on practitioner-administered drugs in the practitioner’s office and on pharmacy billing to the appropriate party, whereas the provider manual specifically addresses home infusion treatments and recognizes both medical and pharmacy benefit pathways. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12, 19 — 2025-10-01]

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion and home infusion drugs/supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit; this newer provider manual should guide current billing questions by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-05-01 is older and focuses on enrollment/billing responsibility for covered products, so it is background rather than the most current home-infusion-specific guidance [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023].
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not specifically define home infusion drugs, but it does state that prescribers must be enrolled for NYS Medicaid to cover products and that NYRx will pay for covered products and services when the prescriber is enrolled in NYRx: "Providers who prescribe drugs and supplies for NYS Medicaid members and/or MMC enrollees must be enrolled in NYS Medicaid as either an ‘Individual Billing’ provider or as an ‘Ordering, Prescribing, Referring, or Attending’ (OPRA) provider for NYS Medicaid to cover the products. NYRx will pay for covered products and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023]. Older policy guidance also says billing responsibility rests with the pharmacy: "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance directly addresses home infusion: "The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]. The manual also says Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as drugs requiring administration by a health care provider and that they may be available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit: "NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources tie coverage to the correct benefit/billing pathway rather than a single bundled payment: the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies "must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], and policy says NYRx pays for "covered products and services" when the prescriber is enrolled in NYRx [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023].

Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider side: the manual requires home infusion drugs/supplies to be billed through the proper benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], and policy states "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current on home infusion coverage, stating there is "no bundled payment" and that home infusion drugs/supplies "must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically define home infusion drugs [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023].

The policy excerpts focus on enrollment and general billing responsibility—"must be enrolled in NYS Medicaid" and "the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]—whereas the provider manual gives the current home-infusion-specific coverage rule and benefit routing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources tie coverage to the correct benefit/billing pathway rather than a single bundled payment: the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies "must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], and policy says NYRx pays for "covered products and services" when the prescriber is enrolled in NYRx [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023].

Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider side: the manual requires home infusion drugs/supplies to be billed through the proper benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], and policy states "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Prescribers and Enrollment
Providers who prescribe drugs and supplies for NYS Medicaid members and/or MMC enrollees must be
enrolled in NYS Medicaid as either an “Individual Billing” provider or as an “Ordering, Prescribing, Referring,
or Attending” (OPRA) provider for NYS Medicaid to cover the products. NYRx will pay for covered products
and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx. There are two paths
of enrollment, as a biller or OPRA privder:
Physicians, Nurse Practitioners (NPs), Physician Assistants (PAs), Podiatrists, Dentists,
Optometrists, Audiologists, and Certified Nurse Midwives Providers who wish to:
1. Enroll and receive payment for covered NYS Medicaid services should apply as an “Individual Billing
Medicaid”. Information regarding how to enroll is available on the eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and
Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/; or
2. Enroll as an OPRA provider (non-billing) should alternatively enroll as an OPRA provider on the
eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/
info/ProviderEnrollment/.

policy.text:
Multiple patient use
If a single-use vial is utilized for multiple patients, bill for the actual administered drug (per mgs) only.
Payment for drugs furnished by practitioners to their patients is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner
of the drug dose administered to the patient. For all drugs furnished in this fashion, it is expected that the
practitioner will maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug, including the number
of doses of the drug represented on the invoice.

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

test_id_53.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion and home infusion drugs/supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit; this newer provider manual should guide current billing questions by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-05-01 is older and focuses on enrollment/billing responsibility for covered products, so it is background rather than the most current home-infusion-specific guidance [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023].", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt does not specifically define home infusion drugs, but it does state that prescribers must be enrolled for NYS Medicaid to cover products and that NYRx will pay for covered products and services when the prescriber is enrolled in NYRx: \"Providers who prescribe drugs and supplies for NYS Medicaid members and/or MMC enrollees must be enrolled in NYS Medicaid as either an ‘Individual Billing’ provider or as an ‘Ordering, Prescribing, Referring, or Attending’ (OPRA) provider for NYS Medicaid to cover the products. NYRx will pay for covered products and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023]. Older policy guidance also says billing responsibility rests with the pharmacy: \"It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance directly addresses home infusion: \"The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]. The manual also says Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as drugs requiring administration by a health care provider and that they may be available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit: \"NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources tie coverage to the correct benefit/billing pathway rather than a single bundled payment: the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies \"must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], and policy says NYRx pays for \"covered products and services\" when the prescriber is enrolled in NYRx [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023].", "Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider side: the manual requires home infusion drugs/supplies to be billed through the proper benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], and policy states \"It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific and current on home infusion coverage, stating there is \"no bundled payment\" and that home infusion drugs/supplies \"must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not specifically define home infusion drugs [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023].", "The policy excerpts focus on enrollment and general billing responsibility—\"must be enrolled in NYS Medicaid\" and \"the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]—whereas the provider manual gives the current home-infusion-specific coverage rule and benefit routing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]."]}
test_id_53.ground_truth:
The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_54.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_54.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for home infusion drugs and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 still shows Medicaid coverage for certain administered drugs, but the provider manual more specifically addresses home infusion billing and benefit handling. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022]
answers.policy_definition: Policy excerpts say NYS Medicaid FFS covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] The policy excerpts also place billing responsibility on the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says home infusion is not paid as a bundled package: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It further states that “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also recognizes these drugs may be available through either benefit: “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie coverage to drugs administered by a health care professional/practitioner rather than a general bundled home-infusion package: “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] and “home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources require billing to the correct payer/benefit rather than assuming Medicaid is always primary: “bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] and “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current on home infusion coverage, stating there is no bundled payment and that drugs/supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit: “does not provide a bundled payment” and “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt speaks more generally about coverage for administered drugs in the practitioner’s office [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022].

The policy excerpt explicitly assigns billing responsibility to the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], whereas the provider manual excerpt provided here does not assign billing responsibility to a specific party and instead focuses on benefit type and no bundled payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
comparison: Both sources tie coverage to drugs administered by a health care professional/practitioner rather than a general bundled home-infusion package: “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] and “home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources require billing to the correct payer/benefit rather than assuming Medicaid is always primary: “bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] and “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific and current on home infusion coverage, stating there is no bundled payment and that drugs/supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit: “does not provide a bundled payment” and “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt speaks more generally about coverage for administered drugs in the practitioner’s office [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022].

The policy excerpt explicitly assigns billing responsibility to the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], whereas the provider manual excerpt provided here does not assign billing responsibility to a specific party and instead focuses on benefit type and no bundled payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
