answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for home infusion drugs and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-10-01 addresses coverage for drugs administered in a practitioner’s office, while the provider manual specifically says home infusion drugs and supplies are not bundled and must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit items.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says NYS Medicaid FFS covers “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by the listed compendia, and if there is no recognized indication, an approved IRB protocol may be used [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. Older policy language also states that “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. For billing responsibility, the policy excerpts say “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” and that both the facility and the pharmacy must actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for patients [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says, “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” and that “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for “certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” to be available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, subject to pharmacy-benefit eligibility criteria [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources treat infusion-type drugs as potentially covered under Medicaid when the drug meets coverage criteria, rather than as an automatic bundled home-infusion package [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources place billing responsibility on the billing entity/provider side rather than describing a single bundled Medicaid payment for home infusion services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is more specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit,” while the policy excerpts focus on drugs administered in a practitioner’s office and do not specifically define home infusion billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The policy excerpts assign billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and say it must bill “timely and to the appropriate party,” whereas the provider manual excerpt does not assign the billing duty to a specific party in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources treat infusion-type drugs as potentially covered under Medicaid when the drug meets coverage criteria, rather than as an automatic bundled home-infusion package [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources place billing responsibility on the billing entity/provider side rather than describing a single bundled Medicaid payment for home infusion services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is more specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit,” while the policy excerpts focus on drugs administered in a practitioner’s office and do not specifically define home infusion billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The policy excerpts assign billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and say it must bill “timely and to the appropriate party,” whereas the provider manual excerpt does not assign the billing duty to a specific party in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
This could
involve prior authorization (PA) requirements, appeal processes, or changes to medications ordered
to align with the primary plan formulary products, etc. Providers should refer to the Reminder: Pharmacy
Billing for Medicaid Patients with other Coverage article published in the November 2021 issue of the Medicaid
Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_
nov21_pr.pdf, for more information regarding this topic. If all attempts for coverage have been exhausted and
coverage has not been granted from the primary insurer, NYS DOH may consider the denial under special
circumstances. The medication in question would still be subject to any editing requirements under the NYS
Medicaid FFS program. Submitting Third Party Liability (TPL) -covered claims that incorrectly bypass the TPL
Plan responsibility of payment is considered inaccurate billing and may be subject to audit recoveries.
Questions and Additional Information:
• All questions regarding this update should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit by
telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.

Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_55.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for home infusion drugs and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-10-01 addresses coverage for drugs administered in a practitioner’s office, while the provider manual specifically says home infusion drugs and supplies are not bundled and must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit items.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says NYS Medicaid FFS covers “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by the listed compendia, and if there is no recognized indication, an approved IRB protocol may be used [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. Older policy language also states that “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. For billing responsibility, the policy excerpts say “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” and that both the facility and the pharmacy must actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for patients [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says, “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” and that “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for “certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” to be available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, subject to pharmacy-benefit eligibility criteria [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources treat infusion-type drugs as potentially covered under Medicaid when the drug meets coverage criteria, rather than as an automatic bundled home-infusion package [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources place billing responsibility on the billing entity/provider side rather than describing a single bundled Medicaid payment for home infusion services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit,” while the policy excerpts focus on drugs administered in a practitioner’s office and do not specifically define home infusion billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].", "The policy excerpts assign billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and say it must bill “timely and to the appropriate party,” whereas the provider manual excerpt does not assign the billing duty to a specific party in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]."]}
test_id_55.ground_truth:
The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_56.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for how naloxone is reimbursed and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-04-01 is consistent on core billing/reimbursement points, but it is older than the manual.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says that for naloxone, “the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” and that “for the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code ‘96372’ and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:pg. 2 — 2024-04-01]. Earlier policy excerpts also state that NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone “when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:pg. 4 — 2024-02-01] and that it “now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed ... or dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:pg. 7 — 2023-09-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt states that “the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” and that “for the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also says that if billing for both drug and administration, “these claims must be submitted separately” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources say the drug is reimbursed when billed with NDC/reimbursement methodology: “the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:pg. 2 — 2024-04-01] and the same wording appears in the manual [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources address administration billing separately from the drug: the policy says “for the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code ‘96372’” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:pg. 2 — 2024-04-01], and the manual says “for the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is newer and adds a clearer billing instruction that “these claims must be submitted separately” when billing both the drug and administration [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt does not state that separation requirement in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:pg. 2 — 2024-04-01].

The policy excerpts include OTC coverage conditions for naloxone—“when prescribed ... or dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:pg. 4 — 2024-02-01]—but the provider manual excerpt provided here focuses on reimbursement mechanics and does not state those OTC eligibility conditions in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources say the drug is reimbursed when billed with NDC/reimbursement methodology: “the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:pg. 2 — 2024-04-01] and the same wording appears in the manual [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources address administration billing separately from the drug: the policy says “for the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code ‘96372’” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:pg. 2 — 2024-04-01], and the manual says “for the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual is newer and adds a clearer billing instruction that “these claims must be submitted separately” when billing both the drug and administration [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt does not state that separation requirement in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:pg. 2 — 2024-04-01].

The policy excerpts include OTC coverage conditions for naloxone—“when prescribed ... or dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:pg. 4 — 2024-02-01]—but the provider manual excerpt provided here focuses on reimbursement mechanics and does not state those OTC eligibility conditions in the quoted text [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
3. Reimbursement will be made in accordance with existing Medicaid policy related to supervision
and billing rules and requirements.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Special Edition Volume 35 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs, which can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
• Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual
Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN).
Smoking Cessation Policy
• Smoking cessation therapy consists of certain prescription and non-prescription agents.
• Some smoking cessation therapies may be used together. Professional judgment should be exercised when
dispensing multiple smoking cessation products.
• For all smoking cessation products, the member must have a prescription or fiscal order.
• NY Medicaid reimburses for over-the-counter nicotine patches included on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
Emergency Contraception Drug Policy
Both prescription and OTC Emergency Contraception are a covered benefit for all Medicaid members without age
restriction. This includes individuals enrolled in the Family Planning Benefit Program.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_56.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
test_id_56.pm_ground_truth: The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.
test_id_56.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_57.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."

For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-04-01 still shows naloxone is reimbursed when billed under the stated pharmacy/OTC rules, but the provider manual adds the current billing detail that the medication is reimbursed using NDC and reimbursement methodology. [Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — 2024-04-01]
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — 2024-02-01] When dispensing naloxone OTC via a standing order, the excerpts require that the member specifically requested the item on the date of service or the pharmacist made a pharmacist-initiated dispensing based on a determination of need/risk on DOS, that the pharmacy submit one course of therapy (two doses) with no refills, and that the item be dispensed according to FDA guidelines, NYS laws/rules/regulations, and NYS Medicaid requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:5 — 2024-02-01] The newest policy excerpt also states that for the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and that the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — 2024-04-01]

Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines;
NYS laws, rules, and regulations; and
NYS Medicaid Policy.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Policy and Billing Guidance for
crizanlizumab-tmca (ADAKVEO®), New Coverage Criteria and “J” Code
Effective July 1, 2020, New York State (NYS) Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) will begin utilizing criteria to
determine the approval of crizanlizumab-tmca (ADVAKVEO®) for members with sickle cell disease to reduce
the frequency of vasoocclusive crises when the member meets the criteria outlined in this policy. ADAKVEO®
was approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for use on November 15, 2019.
NYS FFS Coverage Policy
In accordance with FDA indications, FFS will reimburse for ADVAKVEO® when the following criteria are met:
• The patient must have a confirmed diagnosis of sickle cell disease
• The patient must be sixteen (16) years of age and older
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting.
• Facilities administering ADVAKVEO® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-04-01 and the provider manual both say naloxone is reimbursed when billed as a pharmacy claim, but the policy excerpt is more specific about OTC coverage while the manual excerpt is more specific about separate billing for drug and administration.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt says the pharmacy will be reimbursed for naloxone when the medication is billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using the reimbursement methodology, and it also says OTC naloxone is covered when prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order: “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024] “New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7-8 — Sep 01, 2023] The older December 2022 update only identifies naloxone as “the life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose” and does not add reimbursement detail. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14:1 — Dec 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt says naloxone-related claims are reimbursed when billed separately for the drug and administration, with the drug billed under the ordering prescriber’s NPI and reimbursed via NDC methodology: “If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] “For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says administration is billed with procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources say the medication is reimbursed when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie reimbursement to pharmacy billing instructions rather than describing a separate special payment amount in the excerpted text. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpts explicitly add OTC coverage conditions for naloxone—either prescribed by a practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order—while the provider manual excerpt does not mention OTC standing-order coverage. [New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14:7-8 — Sep 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
December 2022
Volume 38 | Number 14
Opioid Overdose Prevention
The New York State (NYS) Department of Health (DOH) has recently released two brochures on Naloxone,
the life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose, for individuals who are pregnant or who’ve
recently given birth, as well as their support person(s):
• Patient education brochure tilted Do You or Someone You Know Take Opioids?
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2023 Volume 39 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_59.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
test_id_59.pm_ground_truth: The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for how naloxone is reimbursed and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source is dated 2024-04-01 and is consistent on the core billing rule: naloxone is reimbursed when billed by NDC for the drug, with separate billing for administration using procedure code 96372. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024]

It also says that for administration, "the claim must be submitted using procedure code '96372' and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field" and that "A pharmacy will be reimbursed" for the administration when billed that way. Earlier policy excerpts add that NYS Medicaid covers naloxone OTC when prescribed by the practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, but they do not add a different reimbursement method. policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

These orders must be

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A pharmacy claim
will pay when more than 75 percent of the previously dispensed amount has been used, or up to a 10-day supply of
medication is remaining of the cumulative amount that has been dispensed over the previous 90 days (the more
stringent rule will apply). Therefore, members may refill their prescription(s) early, allowing for an ample supply of their
medication(s) on hand.
Early filling of more than the allowed amount for vacation or a temporary absence, as stated above, is not permissible.
Long Term Care Pharmacy Billing for Residents-Special Considerations

The following two sections, New Patient and Leave of Absence, apply to Long Term Care Pharmacies servicing
Medicaid members in a Private Skilled Nursing Facility, Public Skilled Nursing Facility, Private Health Related Facility, or
Public Health Related Facility, when "NH" or, “N1”, “N2”, “N3”, “N4”, “N5”, “N6”, “N7”, “N8”, or “N9” returns on eligibility
response. The following guidance is not intended for community pharmacies, or those servicing facility types not as
described above.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Earlier policy excerpts add that NYS Medicaid covers naloxone OTC when prescribed by the practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, but they do not add a different reimbursement method. test_id_60.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
